This creates a pincer move on American growers: a massive drop in demand from the largest buyer and an unbeatable price advantage for the primary competitor. The "Input Trap": Energy and Geopolitics As if market share loss weren't enough, the recent escalation in the Middle East has sent shockwaves through the farm ledger. With crude oil spiking toward $81 per barrel and threats to the Strait of Hormuz intensifying, the cost of the "invisible inputs"—diesel and fertilizer—is surging. Urea prices have jumped to $620 per ton (up from $530 last year), and anhydrous ammonia has climbed to $760 per ton. These spikes are fueling a new wave of ag-inflation, ensuring that even if commodity prices stabilize, the cost of production remains dangerously high for the American farmer. The Sentiment Slump The numbers are reflected in the mood of the producers. The Purdue/CME Group Ag Economy Barometer for February 2026 came in at 116—while a minor rebound from January, it remains a staggering 45 points below its February 2025 reading. Nearly 48% of producers now expect "bad times" for the industry, double the share from a year ago.
author: Know Your Commodity

title: Palm Oil’s New Price Floor: Indonesia’s Strategic Tariff Hike!!
excerpt: The global edible oil market just received a significant price signal from the world’s largest producer. Indonesia’s Trade Ministry has officially set the Crude Palm Oil (CPO) reference price for March 2026 at $938.87 per metric tonne. This represents a 2.22%...
body:
The global edible oil market just received a significant price signal from the world’s largest producer. Indonesia’s Trade Ministry has officially set the Crude Palm Oil (CPO) reference price for March 2026 at $938.87 per metric tonne. This represents a 2.22% climb from February’s reference of $918.47, reflecting a tightening global balance and surging demand from key importers. However, for global buyers, the "sticker shock" isn't just in the reference price—it’s in the aggressive escalation of the export tax structure that follows it. The Tax Escalation Under Indonesia’s tiered tariff system, crossing the $938 threshold has triggered a massive jump in fiscal costs for exporters. The specific export duty for March has been hiked to $124 per tonne, a nearly 68% increase from the $74 duty applied in the previous month. When you layer on the separate 10% export levy—calculated as a distinct charge on the reference price—the total fiscal burden per tonne exported becomes a formidable barrier to cheap supply. This tax strategy is a clear signal: Indonesia is prioritizing domestic price stability and the funding of its massive "downstream" industrialization and biodiesel programs. Demand Drivers: India, China, and the Biofuel Push The rise is fundamentally supported by a surge in demand from India and China. As these nations replenish stocks, they are competing with Indonesia's own internal consumption goals. The biodiesel mandates require a massive portion of domestic CPO to stay within Indonesian borders, effectively reducing the surplus available for the global market. This tension between

food security in South Asia and energy security in Southeast Asia is creating a durable floor for prices, even as other vegetable oils face volatility. Strategic Implications for Global Trade For the global agribusinesses, this realignment is a wake-up call. Importers must now navigate a landscape where the "Indonesia Discount" is rapidly vanishing. The higher export duty and levy will inevitably be passed down the supply chain, impacting everything from consumer packaged goods to industrial feedstocks. Furthermore, this move reinforces Indonesia's role as a "price maker" rather than a "price taker," using fiscal policy to steer the global market toward its own long-term economic goals of value-added processing and renewable energy leadership. Conclusion The March 2026 reference price isn't just a number; it is a manifestation of Indonesia’s inward looking trade policy. As the cost of "originating" palm oil rises, the industry must prepare for a period of sustained high-basis levels. The question for 2026 is no longer whether palm oil will remain affordable, but how global supply chains will adapt to a producer that is increasingly focused on keeping its "green gold" at home.
author: Know Your Commodity

title: Global Shipping Gridlock: The Collapse of Middle East Trade!!
excerpt: There has been a rapid and extensive collapse of global shipping operations following the 28 February 2026 US–Israel strikes on Iran and the subsequent closure of the Strait of Hormuz. By 5 March 2026, maritime insurance withdrawals and indefinite booking...
body:
There has been a rapid and extensive collapse of global shipping operations following the 28 February 2026 US–Israel strikes on Iran and the subsequent closure of the Strait of Hormuz. By 5 March 2026, maritime insurance withdrawals and indefinite booking suspensions have effectively severed the Middle East Gulf (MEG) from global container networks. Phase 1: Initial Response (28 Feb – 1 Mar)🚢 A.P. Moller - Maersk: Suspended all ship transits through the Strait of Hormuz on 1 March, citing the absolute priority of crew and vessel safety. Hapag-Lloyd AG: Announced a War Risk Surcharge (WRS) of USD 1,500 per TEU on 1 March, effective immediately for all bookings to the Arabian and Persian Gulf. CMA CGM: Instructed all vessels inside or bound for the Gulf to proceed to designated shelter areas on 28 February and suspended all Suez Canal transits in favor of the Cape of Good Hope. Phase 2: Booking Suspensions & Diversions (2 Mar)⚠️ MSC Mediterranean Shipping Company: Declared an unprecedented “End of Voyage” for all containerized cargo destined for the Arabian Gulf, discharging containers at safe hubs like Salalah or Jeddah. Hapag-Lloyd: Formally suspended all vessel transits through the Strait due to the "security closure" by Iranian authorities. Global Impact: Tanker movements through the Strait dropped sharply, with major crude tankers becoming effectively absent from recorded transits. Phase 3: Total Regional Halts (3–5 Mar)⛽ COSCO SHIPPING: Suspended all services to and from Gulf countries, including the UAE, Bahrain,

Saudi Arabia, Iraq, and Kuwait, on 4 March. Services to Fujairah and Khor Fakkan (outside the Strait) remained technically accessible. Maersk Line, Limited: On 4 March, expanded its suspension to all new cargo bookings from GCC. Insurance Collapse: Major maritime insurers cancelled all war-risk cover for the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz, effective 5 March. The Legal Trap for Shippers⚖️ From a legal standpoint, shippers are facing an uphill battle. Carriers are widely invoking "Liberty Clauses" and "Force Majeure" provisions to justify terminating voyages at intermediate ports or additional costs. Under maritime law, these clauses allow carriers to deviate or end services without liability when hostilities create imminent threats. With insurance cover withdrawn, continued transit is technically "impossible," meaning the burden of on-carriage costs and risk for "abandoned" cargo now falls squarely on the cargo owners. Economic and Logistics Impact📈 Stranded Vessels: At least 37 Indian-flagged ships with over 1,100 seafarers were reported stranded in Gulf waters by 4 March. Transit Delays: Rerouting via the Cape of Good Hope is adding 10–14 days to transit times and significantly increasing fuel consumption and operational costs. The maritime industry has entered a phase of high-uncertainty operations, with carriers prioritizing the safety of seafarers. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_global-shipping-gridlock-the-collapse-of- activity-7435211368909156353-tgXR
author: Know Your Commodity

title: Geopolitical Pragmatism: The Russian Waiver a Step for Global Energy Stability!!
excerpt: The global energy map was redrawn on March 6, 2026, as the U.S. Treasury Department issued a narrowly tailored 30-day waiver allowing Indian refiners to purchase Russian crude oil currently "stranded at sea". This strategic "release valve" is intended to...
body:
The global energy map was redrawn on March 6, 2026, as the U.S. Treasury Department issued a narrowly tailored 30-day waiver allowing Indian refiners to purchase Russian crude oil currently "stranded at sea". This strategic "release valve" is intended to prevent a systemic energy shock amidst the escalating Middle East conflict and the functional closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The Energy Shock: Price Surge in Real-Time As of late today, global energy benchmarks are flashing deep red, reflecting the intense risk premium from regional instability: WTI Crude: Has surged to $85.59, marking a sharp 5.65% increase. Brent Crude: Is trading at $88.92, up 4.11% as the market grapples with potential infrastructure damage at key refineries. Murban Crude: Often a proxy for Middle Eastern grade stability, it has spiked to $99.53, up 5.31%. Natural Gas: Has also climbed to $3.066, reflecting a broader energy-wide inflation spike. India’s Demand Pivot: A Global Stabilizer The core of the waiver’s logic lies in India's role as a "buffer" for global demand. As the world’s fourth-largest refining hub, India normally imports nearly 88% of its crude requirements. By allowing India to absorb approximately 9.5 million barrels of Russian oil currently in transit, Washington is effectively reducing competition in the broader spot market. This "demand reduction" is a critical cooling mechanism. When India secures these prompt Russian cargoes, it withdraws its massive appetite from other non-sanctioned origins, preventing a bidding war that would otherwise drive Atlantic and Middle Eastern benchmarks even higher.

Strategic Offtake and Reserve Buffers India has moved aggressively to capitalize on this 30-day window, with refiners having opportunity of securing Russian crude to offset West Asian shortfalls. For a nation with 74 days of total net imports as reserves, these "stranded" barrels are not just a discount opportunity; they are a strategic necessity for maintaining national energy security while the Strait of Hormuz remains a high-risk combat zone. Conclusion: A Regime of Volatility The March 6 waiver confirms that in a wartime economy, inflation control and energy liquidity often supersede long-term sanctions policy. While the U.S. ultimately seeks to transition India toward record-level American exports, the immediate priority is preventing a global price spiral that would cripple major economies. For global trade participants, this maneuver highlights a new reality where "prompt" cargoes and geopolitical waivers are the only shields against a total supply chain freeze. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_energysecurity-oilmarkets-indiarefining- activity-7435667745876119552-0W33
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Rupee’s Gravity-Defying Descent!!
excerpt: One must look past the daily market noise and focus on the structural "fault lines" currently shifting beneath the Indian economy. The breach of the 92.00 level on March 4, 2026, was not a fluke; it was a signal. If the current Middle East conflict persists,...
body:
One must look past the daily market noise and focus on the structural "fault lines" currently shifting beneath the Indian economy. The breach of the 92.00 level on March 4, 2026, was not a fluke; it was a signal. If the current Middle East conflict persists, we are not just looking at temporary volatility—we are looking at a fundamental re-rating of the Rupee. Is there an economic case for why 94.00 is the immediate station and 98.00 is the year-end destination? 1. The Energy-CAD Death Spiral India’s Achilles' heel has always been its energy dependency. With 40%+ of crude imports originating from a now-volatile Middle East. Current Account Deficit (CAD): As crude prices climbed toward $90, the trade deficit widens mechanically. A sustained $10 increase in oil prices typically adds nearly $14 billion to India's CAD. The Short-Term Shock (Target 94): As refiners scramble for "prompt" cargoes once the US- Russian oil waiver expires on April 4, the sudden demand for Dollars will likely push the pair to 94 within weeks. 2. The "Safe Haven" Vacuum In a "Permacrisis," capital does not seek yield; it seeks safety. DXY Dominance: The US Dollar is currently the world’s only "fire shelter." As global investors exit emerging markets to park capital in US Treasuries, the INR faces a relentless "sell-side" pressure.

Yield Differentials: Even with a hawkish RBI, the narrowing gap between Indian and US yields makes the carry trade increasingly unattractive. 3. The Limits of RBI Intervention While the RBI has been praised for creating "intervention space" and managing the slide, a central bank cannot fight gravity forever. Burning Reserves: While India’s war chest is formidable, utilizing it to defend a specific level against a global tide is a losing game. The Strategy Shift: The RBI’s best-day rebound on March 5 was a tactical success, but in an economist's view, they are likely shifting from "defending a level" to "managing the volatility of the fall". 4. The Road to 98: Structural Realignment Risk Premium: A persistent conflict embeds a permanent "geopolitical risk premium" into the currency. Imported Inflation: As the Rupee weakens, the cost of all imports rises, fueling domestic inflation and forcing the RBI into a corner where they must choose between supporting growth or the currency. Year-End Outlook: If the conflict continues to disrupt the global trade routes, the structural outflow of capital, combined with a "higher-for-longer" energy regime, makes 98.00 a conservative projection for December 2026. Conclusion We are witnessing a "regime shift" in the Indian Forex market. The priority must shift from "hoping for a recovery" to "hedging for a new reality." The Rupee isn't just getting weaker; the world is getting more expensive. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_macroeconomics-forexstrategy-inr- activity-7435678586574901248-0G8l

title: The Great Grain Pivot: Why Australian Wheat is Winning Asia, and Where Does India Stand?
excerpt: The geopolitical storm in the Middle East is doing more than just rattling energy markets; it is redrawing the global grain map. As the conflict following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz intensifies, a massive shift in trade flows is underway. The primary...
body:
The geopolitical storm in the Middle East is doing more than just rattling energy markets; it is redrawing the global grain map. As the conflict following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz intensifies, a massive shift in trade flows is underway. The primary beneficiary? Australia. But as Southeast Asian millers pivot toward the "Safe Harbor" of the South Pacific, the question in New Delhi is clear: Is there a window of opportunity for India? The "Safe Harbor" Effect: Australia’s Strategic Edge Recent reports confirm that SEA buyers are aggressively moving away from Black Sea and European origins. The reasoning is purely logistical and financial: The Freight Trap: With war-risk insurance premiums skyrocketing and major insurers canceling cover as of March 5, the cost of moving grain through the Middle East has become prohibitive. Proximity as Policy: Australia’s proximity to Indonesia, Vietnam, and Thailand allows millers to bypass the volatility of the Hormuz chokepoints. In a world of USD 4,000/TEU surcharges and "End of Voyage" declarations, short-haul reliability is the ultimate premium. The Indian Context: A Strategic Dilemma While Australia is currently the "darling" of the region, India is sitting on a potential goldmine. India has seen a record Rabi wheat planting of 33.4 million hectares this season. With harvesting set to begin the timing couldn't be more critical. The Case for India: Geographic Advantage: India offers a shorter, safer maritime route to Southeast Asia compared

to Atlantic or Black Sea competitors. The Rupee Factor: With the INR hovering at the 92 level, Indian wheat is technically more competitive on the global stage. Food Diplomacy: At a time when global supply lines are severed, India has a unique opportunity to utilize its surplus for G2G deals, strengthening regional ties while easing the domestic storage burden. The Hurdles: Balancing Domestic Stability The Indian government remains laser-focused on domestic food inflation. With fertilizer costs surging due to the Hormuz closure and global energy prices hitting USD 90+, the priority remains securing the "domestic buffer" first. Conclusion: A Tactical Window? The "Australian Wheat Darling" phase is a direct result of the Middle East "Black Hole." If the conflict persists—as many economists fear—the world will look to the next closest stable producer. India’s record crop provides a massive strategic cushion. The challenge for policymakers will be to decide if we can transition from a "protective" stance to a "proactive" one, filling the supply gaps in Southeast Asia before other players recalibrate. The grain markets of 2026 are no longer about who has the most; they are about who can deliver with the least risk. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_agriculture-globaltrade-wheatmarkets- activity-7435925465175064576-mD8y
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Fuel Inflation Flare-up: LPG Hiked, Is the Pump Next?
excerpt: The "war premium" has officially arrived at the Indian doorstep. As of this morning, March 7, 2026, the government has announced a sharp upward revision in Liquified Petroleum Gas (LPG) prices. Domestic cylinders are up by ₹60, while commercial cylinders have...
body:
The "war premium" has officially arrived at the Indian doorstep. As of this morning, March 7, 2026, the government has announced a sharp upward revision in Liquified Petroleum Gas (LPG) prices. Domestic cylinders are up by ₹60, while commercial cylinders have seen a massive jump of ₹115. For the Indian consumer and the broader economy, this isn't just a kitchen-budget issue; it is a signal of a deepening energy crisis triggered by the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The Domino Effect: Why Petrol and Diesel are Under Watch As an economist, the question isn't if petrol and diesel prices will follow, but when and by how much. India is currently navigating a high-stakes energy "pincer movement": The $90 Crude Reality: With global benchmarks hitting $90/barrel due to the US-Iran conflict, the under-recoveries for Oil Marketing Companies (OMCs) are reaching a breaking point. The Currency Weight: With the Rupee breaching the 92 level against the USD, the cost of every imported barrel has effectively increased by nearly 8–10% in local terms over the last week alone. Logistical Nightmares: The diversion of tankers via the Cape of Good Hope adds not just time, but significant freight and insurance surcharges. These costs are traditionally passed on to the pump to prevent a fiscal deficit blowout. The Strategic Dilemma for the Government The government is caught between a rock and a hard place. Holding prices steady would require a massive increase in subsidies, further straining a fiscal deficit already under pressure from

war-driven fertilizer costs. Conversely, raising petrol and diesel prices now would ignite a fresh wave of logistics-led inflation, affecting everything from food to manufacturing. What Should Businesses and Households Prepare For? Short-Term Volatility: Expect a "calibrated" increase in fuel prices. The government may look to slash excise duties partially to cushion the blow, but the sheer scale of the crude spike makes a retail price hike almost inevitable by mid-March. Logistics Surcharges: For those in the agrifood and manufacturing sectors, the commercial LPG hike will immediately increase processing and transport costs. The 94-Level Rupee: As fuel demand stays inelastic, the massive dollar outflow to pay for oil will keep the INR under intense pressure, likely heading toward the 94 level in the short term. Conclusion: The End of Cheap Energy? The era of predictable fuel pricing has been suspended. The current LPG hike is the first "smoke signal" of a broader inflationary fire. Until the geopolitical dust settles in the Gulf, the Indian economy must brace for a high-cost energy regime. The question is no longer about the "spark" of war—it’s about how we manage the heat it generates in our own markets. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_the-fuel-inflation-flare-up-lpg-hiked-is- activity-7435947241497858048-oXRW
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Geopolitical Domino Effect: Why Risk is the New Global Tax!!
excerpt: In the global economy, Risk Perception has become the ultimate "tax." The more a route or hub is perceived as vulnerable, the costlier the countermeasures. In the current Middle East conflict, we are witnessing a systematic, geopolitical dismantling of the...
body:
In the global economy, Risk Perception has become the ultimate "tax." The more a route or hub is perceived as vulnerable, the costlier the countermeasures. In the current Middle East conflict, we are witnessing a systematic, geopolitical dismantling of the world's "safety nets." To understand the current cost of trade, one must look at the chronology of how the "unshakeable" infrastructure was tested and, ultimately, compromised: The Chronology of a Chokehold The Jebel Ali Paralysis (Feb 28 – Mar 1): The crisis began not in the water, but at the shore. As the world’s largest man-made harbor and the primary engine of Middle Eastern trade, Jebel Ali was the first to face booking suspensions. When the "anchor" of regional logistics wobbles, the entire global supply chain feels the vibration. The Aerial Lockdown: Following the strikes, major regional airports—vital for high-value "Air-to- Sea" transshipments—faced immediate operational halts. This severed the "emergency valve" for time-sensitive cargo, forcing everything back toward a maritime system that was already under fire. The Closure of Hormuz (Mar 1–2): The unthinkable happened. The Strait of Hormuz was effectively closed. This wasn't just a military move; it was a psychological amputation of the world's primary energy artery. The Collapse of the "Escape Route": For decades, the Gulf tried to outsmart geography by building pipelines and storage hubs in Fujairah to bypass Hormuz. But as drones "tapped the glass" of this

infrastructure, the world realized that even the workaround was within reach. The "escape route" didn't disappear—it just became too expensive to insure. The Real Impact: The Insurance Spreadsheet Global trade don’t run on bravery; they run on risk calculations made by extremely nervous analysts in New York, London, or Singapore. The real drama isn’t just the physical damage—it’s the brand-new line item on the insurance spreadsheet: the "Geopolitical Chokehold Premium." When the "workaround" (Fujairah) becomes as risky as the "bottleneck" (Hormuz), the logic of global trade recalibrates. Shipping companies aren't just rerouting; they are staring at the same question: If the workaround is also vulnerable, what exactly did we work around? Action Point: Analyzing Risk to Find Opportunity For traders and strategists, the lesson is clear: Volatility in geopolitics is the leading indicator of cost. If you don't understand how these pieces move, you cannot analyze the risk. And if you don't understand the risk, you cannot price the opportunity. A trade decision made without a "geopolitical lens" isn't a strategy—it’s a gamble. There are no "safe bypasses" left—only varying degrees of expensive risk. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_geopolitics-globaltrade-riskmanagement- activity-7436381762001424386-vjbK
author: Know Your Commodity

title: Crude Awakening: The Global Ripple Effect!!
excerpt: The global energy landscape has hit a massive tripwire. As of today, March 9, 2026, crude oil has surged to $113 per barrel, marking a fundamental shift in our economic trajectory. This isn't just a standard market fluctuation; it is a "Black Swan" event...
body:
The global energy landscape has hit a massive tripwire. As of today, March 9, 2026, crude oil has surged to $113 per barrel, marking a fundamental shift in our economic trajectory. This isn't just a standard market fluctuation; it is a "Black Swan" event driven by the escalating Middle East crisis. The Global Ripple: An "Energy Tax" on Growth🌍 On a global scale, $113 crude acts as a regressive "inflation tax" on growth. Just as central banks were nearing their 2% inflation targets, this surge threatens to add significant pressure to global CPI, potentially forcing a reversal of anticipated interest rate cuts. Every sustained $10 rise in oil prices can shave up to 0.15 percentage points off global GDP. Beyond the fuel pump, the shipping costs are skyrocketing, impacting the global supply chain for everything from microchips to essential food commodities. The India Perspective: A Macroeconomic Stress Test🇮🇳 For India, the stakes are exceptionally high. As a nation that imports nearly 88% of its crude requirements, we are entering a period of "Imported Inflation" that will test our fiscal resilience to the limit. A sustained $113 price point could widen our Current Account Deficit by an estimated $40 billion, putting immense pressure on the Rupee, expected to test the ₹94 per USD mark. This creates a brutal fiscal trilemma: pass the costs to consumers and fuel domestic inflation, cut excise duties and miss fiscal deficit targets, or increase subsidies and further strain the national budget. The ripple effect across the Indian domestic economy will be profound and immediate. Higher fuel prices translate directly into increased logistics and freight costs, which in turn raises the

price of essentials like milk, vegetables, and consumer goods. While upstream oil companies may see higher realizations, sectors like aviation, paints, and chemicals will see their margins erased by soaring input costs. The FMCG and e-commerce sectors will feel the pinch as delivery costs climb, hitting the Indian middle class's discretionary spending and overall consumption. The Strategic Pivot🚀 However, history shows that these shocks often serve as a necessary catalyst for structural change. We are likely to see an accelerated transition toward electric vehicles, green hydrogen, and ethanol blending as the argument for energy independence becomes undeniable. Resilience is no longer just a corporate buzzword; it is a survival requirement. As we navigate this $113 reality, we must look beyond the immediate crisis toward a more resilient energy architecture. We are at a crossroads where strategic foresight will define the winners of the next decade. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_crudeoil-globaleconomy-indiagrowth- activity-7436600992814714880-Y701
author: Know Your Commodity

title: Climate-Geopolitical Pincer Movement: Grains are Defying Gravity!!
excerpt: The global grain market has just sent a loud, expensive signal to the world. As of March 10, 2026, we are witnessing a synchronized rally in Wheat, Corn, and Soybeans that defies traditional logic. While spreadsheets might show a "huge glut" in global...
body:
The global grain market has just sent a loud, expensive signal to the world. As of March 10, 2026, we are witnessing a synchronized rally in Wheat, Corn, and Soybeans that defies traditional logic. While spreadsheets might show a "huge glut" in global warehouses, the market has moved beyond the simple math of current supply and demand. We are now navigating a Climate-Geopolitical Pincer Movement where the uncertainty of the future harvest is far more influential than the surpluses of the past. 1. The Surge: Defying the Global Glut On paper, global grain inventories are healthy. Yet, the "Big Three" have shattered critical psychological barriers over the last month: Wheat (SRW): Up ~15%, hitting an intraday peak of $6.41/bu—the highest in nearly two years. Corn: Up ~10%, with cash prices hitting nine-month highs near $4.18/bu. Soybeans: Up ~10%, marking a fifth consecutive weekly advance toward $12.13/bu. The market is currently ignoring the physical glut because it is busy pricing in the "Risk of the Unknown." A full warehouse today matters little if the shipping lanes are blocked and the next crop is at risk. 2. Geopolitical Chokeholds: The Delivery Crisis The conflict in the Middle East and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz have turned logistics into a battlefield. The "Workaround" Trap: When hubs and spokes alike are tested, the "Safe Haven" premium evaporates.

The Fertilizer Tax: With urea prices jumping from $516 to $683/ton, the cost of the next crop is being front-loaded. This is forcing a massive 1.5-million-acre shift in the U.S. toward soybeans, effectively tightening the future corn balance before a single seed is in the ground. 3. Weather Stress: The Silent Pincer While the war grabs the headlines, the fundamentals are shifting due to severe weather outlooks in the world’s two largest breadbaskets: USA (Winter Wheat): Ratings have plummeted 22% mom as persistent dryness in the Central Plains threatens to "bake in" lower yields. India (The Terminal Heat Factor): Despite record planting the threat of early "terminal heat" in late March could shrink India’s massive buffer. If temperatures spike during the grain-filling stage, India's role shifts from a potential global supplier back to a protective domestic hoarder. 4. Action Point: Trading Uncertainty, Not Inventory We are entering a "High-Risk, High-Cost" regime. In 2026, the value of a grain isn't determined by how much is in the silo, but by the probability of it reaching the market. Monitor Future Estimates: The market is now reactive to the WASDE and weather models, not historical data. Hedge for Volatility: The "Geopolitical Tax" is the new benchmark. Watch the Currencies: As many oil dependent currencies slides, the landed cost will continue to be volatile. The grain markets are reflecting the fear of what we might lose. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_agribusiness-wheat-corn- activity-7436999704514580480-13NZ
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The $119 to $90 Whipsaw: The Oil Correction is a Geopolitical Mirage!!
excerpt: The energy markets just received a masterclass in "headline volatility." Following President Trump’s recent remarks suggesting a potential de-escalation in the Iran conflict, Brent Crude performed a dramatic gravity-defying act—plummeting from a high of...
body:
The energy markets just received a masterclass in "headline volatility." Following President Trump’s recent remarks suggesting a potential de-escalation in the Iran conflict, Brent Crude performed a dramatic gravity-defying act—plummeting from a high of $119.60 to briefly touch levels below $90 on March 9, 2026. But this sharp correction isn't a trend reversal; it is a temporary "sentiment pause" before a structural revaluation. 1. The Sentiment Trap vs. The Refinery Reality While social media and news tickers were ablaze with the $30 drop, the actual trade volumes told a different story. Refining Pragmatism: No major refinery is taking procurement decisions on impulse or political rhetoric. They operate on delivery cycles, and the physical availability of sweet crude in the Middle East remains severely hampered. Volume Analysis: The dip below $90 was driven by high-frequency paper trading and sentiment- led "railing," but the physical floor remains incredibly solid. 2. The New Energy Bracket: 90 – 105 – 120 We see a clear structural band forming for Brent Crude that will define the rest of 2026. Forget the pre-war averages; we have entered a new price regime: The Hard Floor ($90): Even with the most "optimistic" peace rhetoric, Brent is not going anywhere below $90 for the foreseeable future. The depletion of global storage, production losses, and the disruption of the movement have permanently raised the baseline.

The Band of Averages ($95 – $105): This is where the bulk of the year's trading will settle. It is the price point that balances the "War Risk Premium" with the global economy's ability to absorb cost. The Iron Cap ($120): This remains the upper limit. Any push toward $120 triggers immediate demand destruction and aggressive political intervention, making it a "hard ceiling" for the current cycle. 3. Why the "Long Burn" Favors Volatility Markets are notorious for underestimating the duration of modern "hybrid" wars. This struggle with Iran is centered on Energy Hegemony, and that doesn't resolve with a single statement. 4. The Ripple Effect: Beyond the Barrel The "Correction" to $90 is a mirage when you look at the secondary impacts: LPG & Retail: India’s recent domestic LPG hike of ₹60 and commercial hike of ₹115 are based on the $105 average, not the $90 dip. The Fertilizer Link: With energy-linked Urea prices at $683/ton, the inflationary fire is already "baked into" the next harvest cycle. The Action Point: Trade the Band, Not the Headline The $90 level is not "cheap"—it is the high-tension floor of a world at war. For trade strategists, the focus shouldn't be on the $30 correction, but on the structural revaluation of energy. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_energysecurity-brentcrude- geopolitics2026-activity-7437035601511596033-N4ny
author: Know Your Commodity

title: *Exclusive: Is Gulf Capital Quietly Hedging the U.S?*👑
excerpt: In the wake of the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran and the subsequent retaliation, a quiet but profound shift is occurring within the boardrooms of Riyadh, Abu Dhabi, Doha, and Kuwait City. Several Gulf Sovereign Wealth Funds (SWFs) are currently...
body:
In the wake of the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran and the subsequent retaliation, a quiet but profound shift is occurring within the boardrooms of Riyadh, Abu Dhabi, Doha, and Kuwait City. Several Gulf Sovereign Wealth Funds (SWFs) are currently "stress-testing" their U.S. exposure— not as a radical divorce, but as a hard-nosed response to a war they neither wanted nor controlled. A War Not of Their Making Gulf leaders spent years and billions positioning their economies as safe, predictable hubs for global capital, tourism, and tech. The recent escalation has upended that calculus. Instead of de- escalation, they now face: Infrastructure Vulnerability: Projectiles landing on or close to the "giga-projects" meant to diversify their futures. Logistical Chokeholds: Immediate threats to the regional maritime and aviation corridors. Budget Divergence: Rising defense costs when the capital was earmarked for Vision 2030 and similar growth initiatives. The sting is as much political as it is physical. There is a growing perception in Gulf capitals that Washington made escalation choices that left regional territory exposed while prioritizing its own bases and immediate defense needs. From Frustration to Financial Reassessment This isn't about ideology; it’s about Risk Repricing. The assumption that U.S. assets are the "ultimate safe haven" looks less absolute when the U.S. is the epicenter of a regional conflict. We

are seeing a tactical pivot characterized by: Slow-Walking Disbursements: Indicative "mega-pledges" from pre 2025 are being re-evaluated. If it’s not a binding check, it’s currently being resized or delayed. Liquidity Stress-Testing: SWFs are reviewing their positions in U.S. public markets and private equity—not to dump assets, but to know exactly how fast they can raise cash for domestic cushioning. National Security Clauses: Legal teams are actively reviewing force majeure and security clauses as levers to renegotiate timelines on capital-intensive Western projects. The Leverage Shift: Not a Divorce, but a Repricing Make no mistake: the structural ties between Gulf capital and U.S. markets remain deep. The liquidity, rule of law, and dollar-centrality of the American system aren't easily replaced. However, the tone of the relationship has changed. Capital as a Signal: Gulf rulers are increasingly willing to use capital allocations as a political barometer to signal displeasure to Washington. The Seat at the Table: There is a refusal to write open-ended checks to a system that generates instability on their doorstep without a seat at the decision-making table. The Asia Tilt: At the margin, new money is quietly flowing toward Asia as a strategic hedge. The Bottom Line The "unhappiness" in the Gulf is rooted in a simple economic reality: You cannot ask for a partner's capital while simultaneously jeopardizing their physical security. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/geopolitics2026-sovereignwealthfunds-middleeasteconomy- share-7437071792516661248-Mn3c
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Soymeal Washout: Why Logistics, Not Supply, Is the New Global "Risk Tax"
excerpt: As we observe the current agricultural landscape, we are seeing a dangerous paradox. We have record soybean harvests in South America, yet we are witnessing a "supply chain fracture" that is effectively neutralizing that abundance. As of March 10, 2026, the...
body:
As we observe the current agricultural landscape, we are seeing a dangerous paradox. We have record soybean harvests in South America, yet we are witnessing a "supply chain fracture" that is effectively neutralizing that abundance. As of March 10, 2026, the global agricultural trade is facing its most significant structural test in decades. The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the continued paralysis in the Red Sea have moved beyond "delays"—they are now triggering massive "soymeal washouts" across Brazil and Argentina. 1. The Mechanics of a "Washout" A "washout" occurs when exporters determine that the risk—or the cost—of delivery far outweighs the contract value. The Trend: Major South American exporters are currently cancelling contracts or opting for cash settlements rather than risking vessels through active war zones. The Driver: It isn't a lack of meal; it’s a lack of "insurable passage." When the risk of hull loss or cargo seizure becomes untradeable, the physical supply becomes "marooned." 2. The Corporate Squeeze: The Bunge Case Study The financial toll on global "ABCD" players is becoming visible. Bunge has reportedly faced upward of $1 million in extra costs per voyage due to the necessity of rerouting around the Cape of Good Hope. Fleet Capacity: This detour isn't just expensive; it’s slow. By adding 12–15 days to a round trip, global fleet capacity is being artificially "tightened." Earnings Impact: This logistical friction has forced downward revisions for H1 2026 earnings guidance. When the world’s largest processors begin to struggle with the "Risk Tax," the entire downstream value chain—from poultry feed to food manufacturing—feels the burn.

3. The Impact on Protein Security For nations in Middle East to South/South East Asia these global fractures create a dual-edged sword. The Export Opportunity: With South American meal trapped or expensive, Indian soybean meal (non-GMO) becomes a premium "safe harbor" for Southeast Asian buyers. The Input Risk: However, as the price of global meal "washes out" and rerouting costs are baked into the price, the cost of protein-rich feed will inevitably climb, challenging domestic price stability. 4. Strategic Takeaway: Trade the Risk, Not the Inventory In 2026, the value of a commodity is no longer determined by the silo; it is determined by the shipping lane. Supply is Not Security: A "huge glut" in Argentina is meaningless to a miller in Egypt if the Strait is closed. The New Equilibrium: We are entering a regime where logistics-driven volatility will persist with an upward bias, regardless of harvest size. The "Geopolitical Tax" is no longer an outlier; it is the new benchmark for global trade. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_the-soymeal-washout-why-logistics-not- supply-activity-7437358624126976000-6s4J
author: Know Your Commodity

title: India’s Pulse Policy: Stability, Self-Reliance, and the "Logistics Shield"!!
excerpt: As we approach the fiscal deadline on March 31, 2026, the corridors of power in New Delhi are signaling a "steady-as-she-goes" approach to India’s vital pulse trade. The Government of India is likely to maintain its current import architecture, a...
body:
As we approach the fiscal deadline on March 31, 2026, the corridors of power in New Delhi are signaling a "steady-as-she-goes" approach to India’s vital pulse trade. The Government of India is likely to maintain its current import architecture, a sophisticated balancing act that aims to keep consumer prices in check while ensuring the domestic farmer captures the "Premium" they deserve in a high-inflation world. The Production Cushion: Abundance Meets Resilience The statistical backbone of this policy is the 2nd Advance Estimates for the 2025–2026 crop cycle. The numbers paint a picture of a "comfortable" supply side. Tur (Pigeon Peas) production is estimated at 3.45 MMT, signaling a healthy recovery in the Kharif belts. Gram (Chickpeas) continues to be the anchor of India’s food security at a robust 11.79 MMT, while Lentils (Masoor) hold steady at 1.73 MMT. This domestic cushion allows the government to resist reactive policy shifts, providing the market with the predictability it has craved since the 2022 volatility. The Validated Duty Regime: Decoding the Post-March 31 Landscape The upcoming policy cycle is set to validate a multi-tier duty structure. Tur and Urad will remain on the "Duty-Free" list, a move essential for maintaining the supply as we continue to lean on key global origins. Conversely, Lentils and Desi Chickpeas will continue to attract a 10% Basic Customs Duty (BCD), a strategic floor designed to prevent domestic prices from crashing during the peak harvest arrival. Yellow Peas, which saw a policy flip in late 2025 to prevent market flooding, will maintain a 30% duty, while Kabuli Chickpeas will remain at a steeper 40% duty to

protect local high-value growers. Moong Beans remain strictly in the "Restricted" category to protect the margins of local growers. The "Logistics Shield": Why Freight is the New Tariff In 2026, the most potent protection for the Indian farmer isn't always a government tariff—it’s the "Risk Tax" on global shipping. With the Red Sea disruptions persisting, the cost of bringing a container from Western origins like Canada, the USA or Brazil has surged by 15–20%. The necessity of rerouting vessels around the Cape of Good Hope adds roughly $1 million per voyage in fuel and insurance surcharges. These staggering logistical costs act as a "natural barrier," keeping the landed price of imported pulses high. This ensures that domestic harvest can be sold at reasonable price without the need for the government to impose further restrictive duties. Strategic Takeaway: Supply is Adequate, but the Pipeline is Expensive The 2026 pulse policy is a masterclass in "Passive Protectionism." By leveraging domestic production gains and allowing global logistical chaos to function as a natural import deterrent for long-haul origins, India is securing its pulses supply while shielding its agrarian economy from global price shocks. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_indias-pulse-policy-stability-self-reliance- activity-7437697815847256064-mwTW
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Great Soybean Squeeze: When Geopolitics Hijack the Value Chain!!
excerpt: the Indian soybean sector is navigating a high-stakes survival game. Once the undisputed titan of the non-GMO soymeal export market, India is currently caught in a "double pincer" move— squeezed by domestic crop failures and paralyzed by external maritime...
body:
the Indian soybean sector is navigating a high-stakes survival game. Once the undisputed titan of the non-GMO soymeal export market, India is currently caught in a "double pincer" move— squeezed by domestic crop failures and paralyzed by external maritime chokepoints. The narrative has shifted from simple commodity trading to a sophisticated game of origin intelligence and maritime de-risking. The Domestic Deficit: A Fortress Under Siege The 2025–26 crop cycle has turned into a cautionary tale of climatic and economic volatility. Current projections estimate production at 9.92 MMT, representing a staggering 23% collapse from the 12.9 MMT recorded in the previous year. This deficit is rooted in a significant acreage shift; disillusioned by realizations that lingered 15–20% below the Minimum Support Price (MSP) of ₹5,328/quintal, farmers in key belts like Madhya Pradesh and Maharashtra have pivoted to more profitable alternatives. Compounding this, premature terminal heat in early March 2026 has introduced a secondary yield risk, with heat alerts in the northern belts threatening to shave another 3–5% off the final harvest. The West African Trap: Choking the "Seed-to-Crush" Pipeline India's strategic reliance on West African non-GMO hubs—specifically Niger, Benin, and Togo — has become its greatest vulnerability. These seeds are the lifeblood of India's non-GMO seed deficit. However, the "Hormuz Trap" has set in; with the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz and continued Red Sea paralysis, roughly 150,000 to 200,000 tons of incoming seed cargo are currently "marooned" at sea. Rerouting these vessels around the Cape of Good Hope is a financial

hemorrhage, adding extra costs and pricing, hence driving Indian origin meal out of the global market. The Pivot: Why Latin American Oil Wins Perhaps the most alarming trend is the looming washout of nearly 500,000 tons of soymeal export contracts. When the key export markets vanish overnight and logistics and insurance destroys the margin, exporters choose to settle in cash rather than ship physical meal. This has broken the math of the traditional crush. With meal inventory piling up at Kandla and Mundra ports, depressing local prices, crushing expensive domestic or West African seeds has become economically unviable. Currently, it is significantly more practical to import soybean oil directly from Latin America— where a GMO glut has kept prices competitive—than to crush imported seeds and be left with a surplus of unsellable meal. Direct oil imports allow refiners to bypass the "meal-inventory liability" entirely. Conclusion: Beyond "Supply is Security" The 2026 crisis has proven that "Supply is not Security." https://www.linkedin.com/posts/the-great-soybean-squeeze-when-geopolitics- share-7437711265621495808-4PzS
author: Know Your Commodity

title: Unusual Measures: India Turning to China for Fertilizers!!
excerpt: The global agricultural value chain is witnessing a moment of unprecedented "commodity diplomacy." The conflict in West Asia hasn't just spiked the price of oil; it has paralyzed the primary feedstock for food production: Natural Gas. The recent move by New...
body:
The global agricultural value chain is witnessing a moment of unprecedented "commodity diplomacy." The conflict in West Asia hasn't just spiked the price of oil; it has paralyzed the primary feedstock for food production: Natural Gas. The recent move by New Delhi to approach Beijing is the ultimate signal that we have entered an era where geopolitics now dictates the soil's nutrient profile. 1. The China Request & The Gas Crunch India has approached Chinese officials to ease urea export restrictions. The driver is US-Israeli war on Iran which has severely disrupted LNG flows through the Strait of Hormuz. This has forced Indian fertilizer plants—reliant on imported gas—to slash production. For example, GNFC recently reported a reduction to 60% of its daily contracted gas quantity due to force majeure from suppliers. The China Factor: China, the world's top urea producer, currently restricts exports via a strict quota system to prioritize its own spring planting. As of now, Beijing has yet to allocate any outbound quotas for 2026, making India’s request a high-stakes geopolitical "ask." 2. Demand & Supply: The 180 LMT "Inventory Shield" While the production side is under pressure, the Department of Fertilizers, GoI has confirmed a massive strategic buffer to prevent a domestic panic. Total Stock: India holds 180.12 LMT of fertilizers—a 36% jump YoY. Urea Inventory: 61.51 LMT is currently available. The Gap: With domestic production facing a gas-induced "trim," India has already imported 98

LMT of finished fertilizers and still has contracted another 17 LMT over the next three months to secure the upcoming Kharif (monsoon) season. However, contracted imports hit with logistics hurdles and possible cancellations and domestic production estimated reductions of another 25 LMT, the gap has become wider. 3. The Price Escalation & Subsidy Surge The financial cost of this security is staggering. Imports Urea Price: The imported urea price could touch $700/MT —a historic peak driven by energy costs and maritime risk premiums. The Subsidy Wall: An additional ₹19,230 crore is being sought in supplementary grants for fertilizer subsidies. Total Fiscal Burden: The revised fertilizer subsidy for FY26 could inflate to a massive ₹2 Lakh Crore+. Analysis: Decoupling Energy from Bread India is attempting a masterclass in Decoupling. By capping the MRP of Urea at ₹242/bag and DAP at ₹1,350/bag while import costs could hit $700/MT, the government is essentially absorbing the "War Tax" to protect the farmer. However, the "China Pivot" reveals a deeper truth: Supply is no longer just about the wallet; it is about the "Relationship." If Beijing opens the urea gate, it signals a significant thawing of economic ties; if they don't, the Indian food security goals will face their toughest test ever. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_fertilizercrisis-indiachinatrade- agrieconomics-activity-7438108915130707968-c29Y
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The War Pivot: Why the "Iranian Trap" is a Russian Windfall!!
excerpt: The geopolitical map of 2026 has been redrawn in less than 14 days. As the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict paralyzes the Strait of Hormuz—the world’s most critical energy artery—the Russian economy is experiencing a structural resurgence that few predicted at the...
body:
The geopolitical map of 2026 has been redrawn in less than 14 days. As the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict paralyzes the Strait of Hormuz—the world’s most critical energy artery—the Russian economy is experiencing a structural resurgence that few predicted at the start of the year. What we are witnessing is not just a price spike; it is a fundamental re-valuation of non-Hormuz energy assets. 1. The "Urals Premium" Paradox For two years, the market’s focus was on the "G7 Price Cap" and the "Russian Discount." That era is over. The discount has flipped to a strategic premium. The Price Surge: Brent crude has blasted past the $120/barrel mark once and is consistently above $90, up from $70 in February. Historic Reversal: Russian Urals crude, which previously traded at a $10–15 discount to Brent, is now commanding a $4–$5 premium for Indian and Asian deliveries. With the Persian Gulf effectively "locked," Russian barrels are the only high-volume, "safe-route" alternative available. Budget Windfall: Russia’s 2026 budget was built on a conservative $59/bbl assumption. At current realizations of $90–$95/bbl, the Kremlin is looking at a massive fiscal surplus, with daily fossil fuel revenues already up 7–13% month-on-month. 2. Europe’s "LNG Trap" and the Policy Rethink Europe’s strategy to decouple from Russia relied heavily on Qatari LNG. With the Strait of Hormuz closed, that strategy has hit a wall. The Qatari Shortfall: Roughly 20% of global LNG is currently trapped. The Rethink: European gas storage levels are at a 3-year low (46 bcm). Industry analysts now suggest that Europe may be forced to quietly "re-examine" its anti-Russia energy policy. The

perceived reliability of Russian pipeline gas (via the Druzhba or TurkStream) is suddenly outweighing the "Risk Tax" of a frozen, LNG-starved winter. 3. The Fertilizer & Agri-Export "Turbo" The impact extends far beyond the oil well. Russia is leveraging the "Input Cost Crisis" to solidify its role as the world's primary nutrient supplier. The Urea Shock: Global Urea prices have breached $600/MT, a 50% jump this year. With Middle Eastern plants (Qatar, Iran, Egypt) struggling with gas feedstock disruptions, Russia has stepped in to fill the gap for Brazil, India, and the U.S. Grain Realization: Russian wheat and oilseed exports are seeing higher realizations as global logistics costs make long-haul shipping from Australia or North America prohibitively expensive. Analysis: The Decoupling of the "War Machine" In early March, the US Treasury issued a 30-day waiver for Indian refiners to purchase Russian oil stranded at sea—a tacit admission that global stability now requires Russian volume to prevent a $150 oil shock. For the Russian economy, the "Hormuz Risk" is the ultimate insurance policy. It turns sanctions into suggestions and redirects the world’s trade balance toward the Black Sea and the Arctic. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_energysecurity-russiaeconomy- oilandgas-activity-7438120371440197633-XoGc
author: Know Your Commodity

title: Navigate the Strait of Hormuz Crisis: India’s Strategic Diplomacy!!
excerpt: As we navigate the complex geopolitical landscape, the ongoing crisis in the Strait of Hormuz remains a critical flashpoint for global energy security. Following significant maritime disruption where over 200 vessels were stranded inside the Persian Gulf due...
body:
As we navigate the complex geopolitical landscape, the ongoing crisis in the Strait of Hormuz remains a critical flashpoint for global energy security. Following significant maritime disruption where over 200 vessels were stranded inside the Persian Gulf due to conflict, India has successfully secured conditional safe passage for a select number of its vessels. This breakdown highlights India's unique position and the current status of its maritime operations in this high-risk zone. The Breakthrough: Vessels Granted Conditional Safe Passage Following direct negotiations between India’s External Affairs Minister and his Iranian counterpart, a "rare exception" was granted allowing specific India-bound shipments to transit the Strait. The Shivalik, an Indian-flagged LPG tanker carrying over 40,000 metric tonnes of essential gas, successfully transited and reached open sea as of March 14, 2026. Furthermore, India secured safe passage for the Pushpak and Parimal (both Indian-flagged oil tankers). On March 12, the Liberia-flagged Suezmax tanker Shenlong, captained by an Indian master and carrying vital Saudi crude oil, became the first India-bound shipment to navigate the strait since the escalation, successfully arriving at Mumbai port. Reports also indicate the Indian oil tanker Jag Prakash safely departed the Strait. The Conditional Waiver Framework This "safe passage" is not an open corridor; it operates under a precise, case-by-case framework demanded by Iranian authorities. For each transit, Indian vessels must: Obtain prior approval from Iranian authorities before attempting the passage.

This mechanism reinforces continued Iranian control over the Strait. It is a managed exception, not a broad deregulation. Current Shipping Status and Stranded Vessels Despite these breakthroughs, significant challenges remain. Approximately 24 Indian-flagged ships remain west of the Strait of Hormuz (stranded inside the Persian Gulf). Additional movements, such as the expected crossing of another LPG carrier (likely the Nanda Devi) on March 14, are ongoing. The Humanitarian Focus A critical dimension of this crisis is the humanitarian impact. India remains prioritized on monitoring the safety and security of approximately 778 Indian seafarers currently in the conflict zone. The government has established a 24-hour control room dedicated to their safety. Overall Scenario While many commercial vessels have been confirmed as hit in the Hormuz since the start of the conflict—driving up insurance costs and snarling global supply chains—India’s strategic use of balanced diplomacy has created a vital, conditional corridor for its energy imports. The situation remains fluid, underscoring the vital link between maritime security and national stability in an age of regional volatility. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/straitofhormuz-maritimesecurity-energysecurity- share-7438470907788001280-gbH5
author: Know Your Commodity

title: EU Sugar Policy 2026: The "Bitter" Pivot Toward Protectionism!!
excerpt: As we move through March 2026, the European Union is signaling a seismic shift in its sugar trade architecture. Commissioner Christophe Hansen's announcement that the European Commission intends to suspend duty-free sugar imports for at least one year is more...
body:
As we move through March 2026, the European Union is signaling a seismic shift in its sugar trade architecture. Commissioner Christophe Hansen's announcement that the European Commission intends to suspend duty-free sugar imports for at least one year is more than just a regulatory tweak—it is a strategic intervention to rescue a domestic sector struggling under the weight of a global supply glut and collapsing regional prices. 1. EU Production: A Surplus in Search of a Floor The EU’s 2025–26 sugar beet harvest has been surprisingly robust. Favorable weather in the "Sugar Belt" (France, Germany, and Poland) has pushed production estimates toward 15.8 MMT, a 6% increase year-on-year. However, this bounty has been a double-edged sword. With domestic stocks high, the influx of duty-free sugar has sent white sugar prices in the EU plummeting from their 2024 peaks of €800/t to below €450/t in some regions, threatening the solvency of smaller beet processors. 2. The 700,000-Tonne Curb: Stabilizing the Market The core of the Commission’s plan is to remove roughly 700,000 tonnes of "preferential" sugar from the internal market. The Logic: By suspending duty-free access, the EU is effectively raising a "financial wall" that forces industrial users (confectionery and beverage giants) back toward local beet sugar. The Goal: To lift regional prices back to a "sustainable corridor" of €550–€600/t, ensuring that beet contracts for the 2026 planting season remain attractive to farmers. 3. Tentative Duties: What Can Be Invoked? While the exact "emergency brake" triggers are being finalized, analysts expect the EU to revert

to Most Favoured Nation (MFN) rates or specific SPS (Special Safeguard) duties. MFN Rates: We could see a return to the full duty of €419/t for white sugar and €339/t for raw sugar for countries whose Free Trade Agreements (FTAs) are suspended. Volume Triggers: The suspension will likely be automated based on "Traditional Trade Flows," where any volume exceeding a 3-year average is slapped with a prohibitive tariff. 4. The Impact on Key Exporters This move sends shockwaves through the EU’s traditional suppliers: Ukraine: The most impacted. As a major source of duty-free sugar since 2022, Ukraine’s exports to the EU will now face a hard ceiling, forcing them to find outlets in North Africa or the Middle East. Brazil: Although Brazil primarily trades on the world market, the EU's withdrawal as a high- priced sink for surplus white sugar will marginally increase competition in the "London No. 5" futures market. Strategic Takeaway The EU is moving from a "Trade First" to a "Farmer First" posture in 2026. For global traders, this means the EU is no longer an "easy vent" for surplus sugar. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/sugartrade-agripolicy-eucommission- share-7438522824115404800-_NMv
author: Know Your Commodity

title: Shifting to U.S. Energy: An Economic Suicide Pact for India!!
excerpt: The global energy map is being redrawn. In this chaos, a dangerous narrative is emerging in New Delhi’s policy circles: the idea that India should pivot its energy imports toward the United States to "secure" its supply. Oil and gas imports from the U.S has...
body:
The global energy map is being redrawn. In this chaos, a dangerous narrative is emerging in New Delhi’s policy circles: the idea that India should pivot its energy imports toward the United States to "secure" its supply. Oil and gas imports from the U.S has already creeping up. Let’s be brutally honest: for an economy like India, trading the Middle East and Russia for the U.S. isn’t diversification—it is a Strategic Tax that will bleed the foreign exchange reserves and cripple the industrial competitiveness. Here is the hard-hitting reality of why the "Long-Haul" energy model is a disaster in the making. 1. The Geography of Bankruptcy: The "Freight Multiplier" The first law of energy economics is proximity. A tanker from the Persian Gulf takes roughly a week to hit the Indian coast. In contrast, a voyage from the U.S. Gulf Coast is a month and half odyssey. In the high-risk world of 2026, this five week difference is a financial death sentence. The freight cost for U.S. crude isn’t just higher—it is exponentially prohibitive. We are effectively paying a $5 per barrel "Distance Tax" before the oil even touches a refinery. When you multiply that by millions of barrels, you aren't just buying oil; you are subsidizing the global shipping industry with Indian taxpayer money. 2. The Speculative Blind Spot: The 35-Day "Floating Risk" In a market where prices can swing by $20 in a week due to a single tweet or a drone strike, time is a liability. When an Indian refiner buys U.S. oil, they are forced into a 35-day speculative trap. If we buy at a peak price today, and the market cools while that ship is still crossing the Atlantic, the importer is "underwater" for weeks. This massive lag prevents Indian refiners from being

"agile." We are locking in yesterday’s high prices for tomorrow’s consumption. This "capital at sea" doesn’t just hurt oil companies; it widens our Current Account Deficit (CAD) and puts unnecessary, structural pressure on the Rupee. 3. The Technical Mismatch: Refining for a Diesel Economy This is the most critical factor that often gets ignored. India is a Diesel-First economy. Our trucks, our farms, and our industries run on the middle of the barrel. Russian Urals and Arab Medium crudes are "Medium-Sour" grades—they are the perfect chemical fit for Indian refineries, yielding a high percentage of high-quality diesel. U.S. crude, primarily from the Permian Basin, is "Light-Sweet." It is "clean," yes, but it yields a massive surplus of Naphtha and Petrol—products we already have in abundance or don't need in the same volumes. By shifting to U.S. crude, we are forcing our world-class refineries to work against their design. The Bottom Line: Don't Trade Security for Efficiency Shifting to the U.S. isn't just about changing a phone number on a contract; it's about accepting a higher cost of living for 1.4 billion people. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_shifting-to-us-energy-an-economic- suicide-activity-7438538281635536896-D3hx
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Geopolitical Inflation Trap: Fed Forced into a Strategic Hold?
excerpt: The global economic narrative has shifted from "soft landing" to "combat stations." While in March Headline CPI print of 2.4% Y-o-Y (monthly increase of 0.2%) offered a brief moment of misplaced optimism, it is effectively a lagging relic of a pre-war world....
body:
The global economic narrative has shifted from "soft landing" to "combat stations." While in March Headline CPI print of 2.4% Y-o-Y (monthly increase of 0.2%) offered a brief moment of misplaced optimism, it is effectively a lagging relic of a pre-war world. The ground reality—defined by the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran—has unleashed a "Triple-Shock" pincer that makes the current inflationary baseline unsustainable. 1. The Energy Fuse: An 18% Shock in 20 Days The energy market is no longer pricing in supply and demand; it is pricing in survival. The Volatility Curve: Brent crude climb from a mid-February floor of $67/bbl to a panicked peak of $119. The consolidation at $103 is not a "recovery"—it is a high-altitude plateau that is 54% higher than it was just 30 days ago. The Pump Pressure: Domestic gasoline prices have already surged more than 18% since the outbreak of hostilities. Energy is the primary input for every sector this spike is currently working its way through the "economic plumbing," guaranteeing a massive upward revision in the Q2 reports. 2. The Fertilizer Pincer: The "Invisible" Food Tax While oil grabs the headlines, another structural damage is occurring in the soil. Urea’s Moonshot: As Urea prices hit $600/T, a staggering 34.27% monthly explosion. Y-o-Y, we are looking at a 56.53% increase. The Outcome: The disruption has turned a vital agricultural input into a luxury good. "Food at Home" inflation will remain aggressive and sticky as farmers pass on these historic costs to the consumer or economy.

3. The "Distance Tax": A Global Supply Chain Reroute The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has institutionalized inefficiency. Forcing global trade to bypass the Red Sea for the Cape of Good Hope. This isn't just a longer transit; it is a floating inflation tax. Rising fuel consumption for tankers and skyrocketing war-risk insurance premiums mean that the landed cost of every imported goods is rising in real-time. The Fed’s Paradox: The FOMC Outlook The upcoming Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting on March 17–18, 2026, arrives at a moment of extreme policy fragility. With headline inflation mathematically poised to breach the 3% threshold in the Q2, the Fed’s hands are tied. The Forecast: The Federal Reserve will almost certainly maintain the current interest rate of 3.50%–3.75%. Any hope for a rate cut has been incinerated by the fires in the Middle East. The Fed will be in "wait-and-watch" mode, hoping the geopolitical premium doesn't become a permanent structural fixture. Final Thought The 2.4% inflation of February was a snapshot of a world that no longer exists. The "Middle East Risk" has embedded a high-cost baseline into the global economy that cannot be "interest-rated" away. We are entering a phase where energy and food costs aren't just components of inflation— they are the drivers. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/federalreserve-inflation2026-fomc-share-7438614924265820160- jkaD
author: Know Your Commodity

title: THE WAR IN THE PERSIAN GULF COULD PLUNGE THE WORLD INTO HUNGER!!
excerpt: We are watching a slow-motion food catastrophe unfold in real time — and most people aren't paying attention. When the US-Israel strike on Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz, the world's most critical maritime chokepoint went dark. Over 20% of global oil & gas...
body:
We are watching a slow-motion food catastrophe unfold in real time — and most people aren't paying attention. When the US-Israel strike on Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz, the world's most critical maritime chokepoint went dark. Over 20% of global oil & gas and nearly 49% of traded urea flows through this narrow passage. What began as a geopolitical event is now a direct threat to the global food supply. The Scenario: Crude at $130. Urea at $700. This is not alarmism. This is where markets are heading. Brent crude has already surged from $72 to over $105/barrel. Urea — the primary nitrogen fertilizer for corn, wheat and rice — has jumped from $383/mt in January to $598/mt today, up 52% year-on-year. If Hormuz remains closed through spring planting, $700/mt urea is not a ceiling — it could be a floor. What does this mean on the farm? In the US alone, corn cost-of-production is projected to jump from ~$272/acre to $435/acre — a 60% increase. Fertilizer alone accounts for ~$212/acre in this scenario.The timing of surge is literally the worst possible. 25% of US farmers have not yet purchased fertilizer. The American Farm Bureau Federation (AFBF) has already warned the White House. Grain acres shifting. Lower nitrogen application rates. Smaller yields. Tighter global grain production.

The Price Cascade: From Field to Fork In the US Wheat: $5.80 $8.70/bushel (+50%)→ Corn: $4.60 $6.90/bushel (+50%)→ Rice: $510 $720/tonne (+41%)→ These are not abstract commodity numbers. These are the inputs to bread, pasta, noodles — the daily calories of 4 billion people. The double whammy India faces: commodity shock + currency depreciation Every Indian import pays the price in two ways — the commodity rises in USD, and the rupee buys fewer dollars. At ₹88/$ the pain was bad. At ₹94/$ it's compounded more. Cost of production of khariff crop/imported pulses & oil could increase by 28-42%. Who gets hurt most? Sub-Saharan Africa imports 90%+ of its fertilizer. Egypt — the world's largest wheat importer — is on the financial edge. India risks losing 63% of its nitrogen fertilizer imports from Gulf suppliers. The GCC itself, which imports 77–95% of its staple foods, faces acute grocery price shocks. Drawing the parallel clearly: during the Russia-Ukraine fertilizer shock of 2022, 23 countries imposed food export bans within weeks. We are now in a more severe disruption — with more actors, higher energy prices, and tighter buffers. The 2 Billion People Nobody Is Talking About International Monetary Fund data shows that 2 billion people in the Global South spend 40–60% of household income on food. A 40–60% rise in staple prices is not inflation. It is unaffordability. It is hunger. It is instability. Famine does not need a drought to arrive. It just needs a war, a blocked strait, and $700 urea. The world is not running out of food. It could run out of affordable and available food. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_the-war-in-the-persian-gulf-could- plunge-activity-7439238661956624384-Q9eC
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Great Strategic Stall: Trump-Xi Summit Postponement, A Game of Brinks!!
excerpt: The narrative of the "Beijing summit" is shifting from a scheduled diplomatic reset to a volatile theater of leverage. While President Trump uses the summit as a carrot to force maritime cooperation, Beijing’s position is far from weak. In fact, the current...
body:
The narrative of the "Beijing summit" is shifting from a scheduled diplomatic reset to a volatile theater of leverage. While President Trump uses the summit as a carrot to force maritime cooperation, Beijing’s position is far from weak. In fact, the current "Hormuz Waiver" and China’s dominant grip on critical supply chains have turned this into a contest of who can withstand a global market freeze the longest. 1. The Waiver Illusion: China’s Energy "Safe Passage" While the U.S. frames the Strait of Hormuz as a chokepoint to pressure Xi, the reality on the water is different. The "Blanket Waiver": Iran has validated a standing "Safe Passage" agreement for Chinese and Russian-flagged tankers. While the rest of the world pays "War-Risk" premiums and reroutes around the Cape, Chinese crude continues to flow through the Strait virtually unimpeded. The Strategic Buffer: Because China isn't facing the same shipping "tax" as U.S. allies, Beijing views Trump’s demand for naval intervention not as a necessity for their own survival, but as a U.S. request to do Washington’s "heavy lifting." For Xi, there is no immediate "energy panic" to force a submissive stance at the summit. 2. Tariffs vs. The "Tech-Metal" Shield The U.S. administration frequently uses "Phase 3" tariffs as a bargaining chip, but Beijing has long moved past a defensive posture on trade. The Rare Earth Leverage: China’s counter-leverage isn't just about absorbing tariffs; it’s about the

ability to choke the U.S. defense and high-tech sectors. With a 90% dominance in processed rare earth elements and a vice-grip on the global battery supply chain, Beijing views U.S. tariffs as a manageable cost compared to the strategic damage they can inflict on U.S. industrial capacity. The Supply Chain Stalemate: If the summit is postponed and a "Tariff Snap-Back" occurs, Beijing’s response is likely to be a targeted restriction on critical components. In this scenario, the U.S. risks "Inflationary Suicide". 3. The Postponement Paradox: Who Blinks First? President Trump’s threat to delay the summit until China "helps" with Hormuz is a high-stakes gamble on the U.S. domestic economy’s resilience. A U.S.-China "truce" removes the last remaining hope for a market-calming event. If the summit is pushed to Q3, it signals to the world that the two largest economies are comfortable with a "Long Trade War". The Bottom Line: A Stalemate of Giants Trump is betting that he can trade trade-relief for China’s naval power in the Gulf. Xi is betting that China’s "Hormuz Waiver" and supply-chain dominance make the U.S. more desperate for a deal than Beijing. By postponing the summit, Washington won't just pressure China—it will test the breaking point. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/trumpxisummit-tradewar2026-share-7439314815455477761-f4Ck
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The 150-Basis Point Leap: Is India’s May Inflation Heading Toward 5%?
excerpt: The economic arithmetic of 2026 is currently being rewritten by a brutal energy and logistics ledger. As we look toward the May CPI data, the "calm" of early 2026 is quickly evaporating. While inflation sat in the mid-3.5% range just a month ago, current...
body:
The economic arithmetic of 2026 is currently being rewritten by a brutal energy and logistics ledger. As we look toward the May CPI data, the "calm" of early 2026 is quickly evaporating. While inflation sat in the mid-3.5% range just a month ago, current market indicators and supply chain disruptions suggest a "Shock Realignment in March - May." Based on the explosive rise in input costs, we estimate that India’s CPI could surge by nearly 150 basis points, potentially breaching the 5% threshold by May. This is not a certainty, but a projected trajectory of what happens when a "Conflict Risk Premium" finally hits the retail shelf with limited capability of the government to act. 1. The Raw Energy Shock: From $63 to $142 The primary driver of this projection is the sheer velocity of crude oil price increases. To put the scale of the challenge in perspective, India’s average crude buying cost in January 2026 stood at a manageable $63.08 per barrel. By the end of March 2026, that average has ballooned to $108.23. Even more alarming is the volatility at the peak; during the third week of March 2026, purchase costs touched a staggering $142 per barrel. India imports the vast majority of its crude, this price spike acts as an immediate tax on the entire economy. 2. The Gas Pincer: LPG and LNG Scarcity Beyond liquid fuels, the "Gaseous Economy" is under equal duress. The ongoing conflict in the Persian Gulf has severely throttled the supply of LNG and LPG, which are critical for both industrial and domestic stability. We are already witnessing a significant reduction in supply for commercial operations, leading to "energy rationing" in power-intensive sectors.

The inflationary pressure is already visible as prices for both domestic and commercial users tick upward. If the war continues beyond March—which currently seems highly likely—the scarcity of these fuels will likely transition from a temporary disruption to a structural inflationary floor for the second quarter. 3. The "April Trigger": Post-Election Realignment A significant factor in the May projection is the anticipated "Fuel Catch-up." It is widely believed that the government has deferred aggressive retail fuel price hikes due to the ongoing state elections. However, with the electoral cycle concluding in April, a sharp upward revision in petrol and diesel prices appears inevitable. The "April Trigger" will likely see its most aggressive impact in the May reporting cycle. This is why May, rather than March or April, is emerging as the critical month for the 150-basis point surge. Strategic Conclusion: The Policy Ceiling A 5% inflation print in May would represent a 1.5% jump in just a single quarter, effectively paralyzing the RBI's ability to consider rate cuts for the remainder of the year. We are moving into a phase where "Cost-Push" inflation is being driven by factors entirely outside domestic control. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_the-150-basis-point-leap-is-indias-may- activity-7439905257410699264-Hh2j
author: Know Your Commodity

title: Food Is Not a Sector. It Is Hard Power!!
excerpt: For too long, food and agriculture were treated as “soft” issues — important for rural livelihoods and development, but peripheral to the real engines of global power. That era is over. In 2026, food has moved to the center of macroeconomics, geopolitics, and...
body: For too long, food and agriculture were treated as “soft” issues — important for rural livelihoods and development, but peripheral to the real engines of global power. That era is over. In 2026, food has moved to the center of macroeconomics, geopolitics, and capital allocation. It is no longer just about farm output. It is about inflation, political stability, sovereign risk, trade leverage, and strategic resilience. Capital is beginning to understand before politics that food is not a side story to the global economy but foundations of stability in an unstable century. Those who understand that food is now hard power will shape the future. In the article " *Food Is No Longer a Sector. It Is a Fault Line.* " we dig deep, do share your feedback. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/dpareek_foodsecurity-agriculture-geopolitics- activity-7440254443205427200-Z9Qg
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Great Energy Fracture: Mapping the 2026 Crisis!!
excerpt: The global energy map is being redrawn in real-time, and the ink is being replaced by declarations of Force Majeure (FM). As of March 19, 2026, "Operation Epic Fury" has moved beyond a military campaign into a total systemic failure of the world’s most...
body:
The global energy map is being redrawn in real-time, and the ink is being replaced by declarations of Force Majeure (FM). As of March 19, 2026, "Operation Epic Fury" has moved beyond a military campaign into a total systemic failure of the world’s most critical energy artery. We are no longer discussing "volatility"—we are discussing the structural removal of a fifth of the world’s energy supply. 1. The Quantitative Reality: 20 Million Barrels vs. The Void To understand the gravity of this crisis, we must look at the pre-conflict baseline. In 2025, the Strait of Hormuz was the world's most vital chokepoint, handling an average of 20 million barrels per day (mbpd) of crude and petroleum products—approximately 25% of global seaborne oil trade. On the gas side, the region accounted for 112 billion cubic meters (bcm) of Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) annually, primarily from Qatar, representing 20% of global LNG trade. Today, that artery is effectively a no-go zone. Current intelligence suggests a massive daily disruption: Middle Eastern oil output has plummeted from a pre-war 21 mbpd to roughly 14 mbpd, a sudden 7 mbpd deficit that the world’s spare capacity simply cannot fill. With the Strait restricted, three-quarters of the region's typical exports are physically blocked, creating an overnight energy vacuum. 2. The NOC Collapse: From Qatar to Basra The state-owned giants that anchor the global economy were the first to buckle. QatarEnergy led the retreat on March 4, declaring Force Majeure on all LNG shipments following targeted strikes on the Ras Laffan complex—the world’s largest LNG production system.
