This move alone removed 20% of the global LNG supply, sending spot prices to record highs. Kuwait Petroleum Corp (KPC) followed on March 7, citing the inability to guarantee safe transit

through Hormuz. In Iraq, the Basra Oil Company has seen southern exports effectively hit zero. With storage tanks at 100% capacity and no tankers arriving, production has been forcibly throttled. Meanwhile, Bapco Energies in Bahrain and the UAE’s ADNOC have reported production drops exceeding 50%, with the latter’s offshore facilities largely shut down due to the heightened security risk. Saudi Aramco has also confirmed partial halts at the Ras Tanura terminal and Jubail complex following drone-induced damage. 3. The IOC Retreat: Abandoning the Frontlines The "Big Oil" majors are also in full retreat. TotalEnergies has suspended operations in Qatar, Iraq, and the UAE, impacting roughly 15% of its global portfolio. Shell and BP have declared Force Majeure on contracts linked to Qatari supply and have evacuated personnel from Iraq's Rumaila field. In the Eastern Mediterranean, Chevron was forced to shut down the Leviathan and Tamar gas fields, cutting off critical flows to Egypt and Jordan. 4. The Strategic Fallout We are entering a phase where the "Conflict Risk Premium" is no longer a temporary spike but a permanent floor. If the Strait remains restricted through Q2, the global economy faces a recessionary spiral that cannot be solved by strategic reserve releases alone. For importers, the choice is now between expensive spot-market bidding wars or localized energy rationing. The global energy order of the last 50 years died in March 2026. What comes next will be defined by resilience, not just price. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_energysecurity-oilcrisis2026- activity-7440268339790299136-xBZI
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Energy Vacuum: Cannibalizing the Global Commodity Complex!!
excerpt: The global market is currently witnessing a paradox that defies traditional supercycle logic. While historical trends suggest that a "rising tide lifts all boats," the current 2026 energy shock is doing the exact opposite. The Brent crude is trading at $113...
body:
The global market is currently witnessing a paradox that defies traditional supercycle logic. While historical trends suggest that a "rising tide lifts all boats," the current 2026 energy shock is doing the exact opposite. The Brent crude is trading at $113 per barrel ( 55% since start of war ⬆️ 27th Feb), having recently tested a multi-year high of $119. While these prices are below the "panic spikes", the sustained pressure at the $100+ level is creating a structural Liquidity Evaporation that is sucking the life out of metals and agriculture markets alike. The primary driver of this shift is the Collapse of Petrodollar Recycling. In previous oil booms, high prices led to a massive influx of dollars into the global financial system as oil-exporting nations reinvested their surplus. However, with the Strait of Hormuz blockade significantly throttling the volume of exports, the total dollar revenue entering the system has cratered despite the high price per barrel. The "Petrodollar" is no longer being recycled because the physical flow of oil is restricted. This has created a systemic dollar shortage, forcing a Liquidity Drain where capital is diverted from speculative commodities into essential energy procurement and debt servicing. Metals (Gold, Silver, Copper) are the first to feel this vacuum. Copper, usually the bellwether for global growth, is signaling a sharp recessionary pivot (5.4 $/Lbs 10%). As energy costs paralyze ⬇️ manufacturing in Europe and Southeast Asia, the demand for industrial metals has decoupled from energy prices and begun to sag. Even Gold (4760 $/Oz 10%) and Silver (74.8 $/Oz 20%), ⬇️ ⬇️ traditional "Safe Havens," are struggling to hold their ground. In a global liquidity crunch, traders sell whatever they can—even the "safe" assets—to cover margin calls on energy positions and strengthen their cash reserves in an environment of hyper volatility. We are seeing a "Sell- Everything" mentality as liquidity becomes the only true asset that matters.

The Agriculture Pincer is perhaps the most dangerous manifestation of this trend. While high energy/gas costs have pushed input costs to the moon—with Urea touching $610/MT ( 30%) —⬆️ the prices of Wheat ( 2.5%), Rice ( 1%), Maize ( 6%), Soya ( 0.1%), and Palm Oil ( 8.5%) ⬆️ ⬇️ ⬆️ ⬆️ ⬆️ are beginning to weaken after sharp upside from 27th Feb. This is a classic case of Demand-Side Destruction. As the global "Liquidity Tank" empties, the purchasing power is being decimated. We are seeing a structural softening in Soybeans while Palm Oil emerged strong due to the biofuel premium. However their is collapse in discretionary spending and the inability of trade houses to secure the credit needed to move grains. The disconnect between the energy sector and the rest of the market is widening, creating a scenario where "expensive to produce" no longer translates to "expensive to sell." https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_the-energy-vacuum-cannibalizing-the- global-activity-7440287191198760960-pPQS
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Persian War: A Disastrous Global Realignment!!
excerpt: "Operation Epic Fury" has transcended a regional conflict to become a systemic "Global Reset." The blockade of the Strait of Hormuz is no longer just a naval maneuver; it is a financial guillotine for the West’s allies and a windfall for its adversaries. 1....
body:
"Operation Epic Fury" has transcended a regional conflict to become a systemic "Global Reset." The blockade of the Strait of Hormuz is no longer just a naval maneuver; it is a financial guillotine for the West’s allies and a windfall for its adversaries. 1. The Gulf Attrition: Oasis to Frontline For the UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, and Kuwait, the "Global Hub" model has effectively collapsed. The Economic Void: Tourism and aviation are wiped out; luxury real estate is crashing as international capital flees the combat zone. The Cost of Defense: With oil and gas lifelines physically blocked and infrastructure suffering direct hits, these nations are burning through reserves just to maintain a crumbling defense. 2. Europe’s "Dark Spring" & The Ukraine Sacrifice Europe is facing an industrial winter in March. Natural gas prices have doubled, Crippling manufacturing and heating. NATO’s Ideological Schism: The alliance is fracturing not over energy, but over the level of active participation in the war. The Hardware Pivot: Most critically, the Ukraine war is compromised. U.S. military hardware— from Patriot systems to munitions—is being diverted from the Black Sea to the Persian Gulf. Kyiv is being deprioritized as Washington scrambles to secure the world's primary energy chokepoint. 3. India’s Triple Whammy India is the most exposed major economy, caught in a lethal pincer: Remittance Risk: A massive diaspora in the Middle East is under threat, risking both lives and billions in capital inflow.

Currency Freefall: The INR has weakened to 94, making every trade transaction a fiscal nightmare. Import Dependency: With soaring costs for oil, gas, and fertilizers, India’s food security and industrial growth are staring at stagflation. 4. The Macro-Economic Divide The Global South: Facing a literal struggle for survival as food and fuel become unaffordable luxuries. The Global North: Falling into "Budgetary Holes," as emergency energy subsidies stall the R&D and infrastructure spending needed for long-term growth. 5. The Adversarial Windfall While the democratic alliance bleeds, the disruptors are consolidating: China’s Strategic Gain: Despite a growth slowdown, China is winning. With massive reserves, competitive production, and a geopolitical alignment that grants them "Safe Passage" waivers, Beijing is now the magnet for global capital seeking stability. Russia’s War Chest: Elevated prices are a massive windfall for Moscow, fueling its war machine and providing a lifeline of resources to North Korea, further destabilizing the Pacific. The 2026 world order is now defined by a brutal Siege that rewards the adversaries and drains the friends. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_the-persian-war-a-disastrous-global- realignment-activity-7440398365172948992-cAq2
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Biodiesel Premium: Soybean Oil Tracking the Middle East Energy Surge!!
excerpt: The global commodity landscape in early 2026 is defined by a structural repricing of risk. An escalating Middle East conflict has transformed the Strait of Hormuz into a critical bottleneck, injecting a powerful geopolitical premium into crude oil and the...
body:
The global commodity landscape in early 2026 is defined by a structural repricing of risk. An escalating Middle East conflict has transformed the Strait of Hormuz into a critical bottleneck, injecting a powerful geopolitical premium into crude oil and the wider agricultural complex. This shock is transmitted with force into vegetable oils and fertilizers, reshaping price dynamics for the 2026–27 crop cycle. Crude Oil: From Risk to Repricing Brent crude has surged into triple digits, trading in the low-$110s per barrel in mid-March, roughly 55% higher than last month. The late February military escalations effectively choked a corridor handling over 15 million barrels per day. While prices briefly consolidated near $90, persistent shipping interruptions and insurance dislocations have re-ignited bullish sentiment. Forward curves now cluster in the $110–$115 range, repricing the Middle East risk as a semi- permanent feature of the 2026 landscape. Soybean Oil: Biodiesel as a Transmission Channel This energy volatility has established a direct transmission into agriculture via the biodiesel value chain. As petroleum-based diesel rallies, biofuel profitability expands, intensifying competition for feedstocks like soybean oil. In this regime, soybean oil acts as a high-beta surrogate for crude, with correlations exceeding 0.8. Recent action underscores this: US soybean oil futures are trading in the mid-60s cents per pound. When Brent broke $100 in early March, soybean oil responded with sharp intraday gains. Implied premiums suggest a plausible upside toward the mid-70s cents if crude holds current

levels. High fossil diesel prices mechanically pull vegetable oil into fuel channels, creating a demand floor anchored in the energy complex rather than pure food demand. The “Three Fs”: Fuel, Fertilizer, and Finance The conflict has unleashed secondary pressures across the “Three Fs”. Natural gas benchmarks have moved higher, while nitrogen fertilizer markets confront elevated feedstock costs. Key granular urea benchmarks have gained 30% or more, driving up the cost of production per hectare. If the Hormuz impairment persists, global fertilizer benchmarks could average 15–20% above pre-crisis levels through H1 2026. Tighter, more expensive credit further amplifies the risk of undersupplied markets if farmers scale back planted area due to rising costs. Conclusion Soybean oil has emerged as the primary price-discovery vehicle for the soy complex. As long as a geopolitical premium remains embedded in energy, it will maintain its role as a high-beta expression of the energy trade. This intersection of energy policy and rising input costs represents a structural shift across the agricultural value chain. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_the-biodiesel-premium-soybean-oil- tracking-activity-7440623891611275265-mhex
author: Know Your Commodity

title: POLICY ALERT: India’s Pulses Trade Regime Set for Extension 🚨 Beyond March 2026!!
excerpt: The agri-trade ecosystem is buzzing: The Government of India is expected to maintain the status quo on its current import-export policy for pulses for another full year, extending the mandate beyond March 31, 2026, to March 2027. For industry stakeholders,...
body:
The agri-trade ecosystem is buzzing: The Government of India is expected to maintain the status quo on its current import-export policy for pulses for another full year, extending the mandate beyond March 31, 2026, to March 2027. For industry stakeholders, this is a massive signal of intent. What is driving this decision to keep the duty-free import window open for essential pulses like Tur & Urad, low duty regime for Chana & Masur, medium duty on Yellow Peas while maintaining calibrated export curbs on Moong? The strategic drivers behind this move: 1. Anchoring Food Inflation📉 Pulses are a non-negotiable staple for a vast majority of Indians. With retail food inflation remaining a highly sensitive economic metric, the government is prioritizing price stability. Extending duty-free/low-duty imports ensures a continuous supply pipeline, preventing sudden price shocks and protecting everyday household budgets in current environment of energy driven inflation. 2. Bridging the Demand-Supply Deficit📊 Despite strong agricultural outputs, India continues to face a structural deficit in specific pulses. Domestic consumption steadily outpaces production for Tur, Urad and Masoor. Until significant boost in domestic tonnage, targeted imports remain the most viable pressure valve to balance the scales.

3. Navigating Geopolitical & Supply Chain Shocks🌍 Global supply chains are navigating extreme turbulence. With freight costs spiking and vital maritime routes facing disruptions, importers desperately need long-term regulatory certainty. A one-year extension provides traders the confidence to sign forward contracts with suppliers in East Africa, Myanmar, Canada, Russia, and Australia without the fear of abrupt tariff hikes. 4. Hedging Against Climate Volatility🌦️ Unpredictable monsoon patterns and extreme weather anomalies remain the biggest wildcards for Indian agriculture. By securing an open import channel in advance, the government is building a strategic buffer against potential crop damage or yield reductions in the upcoming seasons due to El Nino. Ecosystem Impact:💡 Global Exporters: Gain a massive, stable, and predictable buyer for another year. Domestic Importers: Benefit from regulatory clarity, allowing for better supply chain planning. Domestic Farmers: Face a complex reality. While imports cool consumer prices, the government must aggressively step up Minimum Support Price (MSP) procurement to ensure Indian growers remain incentivized. The Road Ahead While extending the current policy is a vital tactical maneuver for economic stability, the ultimate goal remains achieving true "Atmanirbhar" (self-reliance) in pulses. This requires aggressively investing in R&D and incentivizing farmers to switch to pulse cultivation. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_tradepolicy-commoditymarkets- activity-7440632388365455360-YVs9
author: Know Your Commodity

title: Fertilizer Economics: Surplus to Structural Collapse!!
excerpt: The escalation in the Persian Gulf has crossed a point of no return. The destruction of the massive shared gas field between Qatar (North Field) and Iran (South Pars) has transformed a maritime logistical crisis into a structural catastrophe for global...
body:
The escalation in the Persian Gulf has crossed a point of no return. The destruction of the massive shared gas field between Qatar (North Field) and Iran (South Pars) has transformed a maritime logistical crisis into a structural catastrophe for global agriculture. We are witnessing the ultimate weaponization of the "Energy-in-Food" nexus. The Great Paradox: Supply-Rich, Trade-Poor The global urea market looked comfortable before the crisis. Total global capacity sits at 240 Million Metric Tonnes (MMT), with a 2026 production forecast of 204.1 MMT against a global consumption requirement of roughly 185 MMT. However now we see a 15% dip converting 19 MMT surplus to an estimated 11.5 MMT deficit. Yet, the delivered (CFR) price in India and Brazil has skyrocketed to a staggering $650–$700/T. The price has jumped to close to $850-$900/T in Australia. Why? Because the traditional 55 MMT international trade volume has been paralyzed by a "Dual Divorce." 1. The Infrastructure Wipeout: The Middle East previously accounted for ~27 MMT of production. Qatar’s QAFCO (5.6 MMTPA) and Iran's export capacity (~5 MMT) were the undisputed crown jewels of this system. With their primary gas feedstock physically destroyed, roughly 10.5 million tonnes of annual export capacity evaporated overnight. 2. China's Nitrogen Wall: China is the undisputed heavyweight, producing ~76.5 MMT (31% of world share). Yet, Beijing is hoarding its 10.5 MMT surplus to ensure domestic availability, issuing zero export permits for 2026.

The "Big Five" Under Siege The nations absorbing nearly 60% of all seaborne urea are now in an existential bidding war for the remaining tradable supply: India (10.0 MMT): The world's largest buyer is in critical danger. With domestic capacity already throttled by LNG shortages, with Qatari supply destroyed and China not entertaining, India is entirely at the mercy of Russian and Omani exports for the upcoming Kharif season. Brazil (7.5 MMT): Relying on Russia and China for over 58% of imports, Brazil is desperately substituting urea with Ammonium Sulphate. Australia (3.8 MMT): Facing a >95% import dependency (historically 64% from the Gulf), Australia must pay exorbitant premiums to secure Southeast Asian cargoes before its winter crop window closes. The Strategic Realignment This physical deficit guarantees severe yield contractions for global cereals in late 2026. Geopolitically, Russia (forecasting 10.5 MMT in exports) is left holding the absolute monopoly on swing supply. Long-term, this vulnerability will force an unprecedented pivot of capital into Green Ammonia and precision ag-tech to permanently decouple global food security from fossil-fuel volatility. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_agrieconomics-globaltrade-foodsecurity- activity-7440666926101716992-OscN
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Mandi-to-Mediterranean Crisis: Kabuli Chana Exports Roadblock!!
excerpt: The Indian Kabuli Chana market is facing a structural "Logistics Siege." Despite a healthy harvest, the physical movement of this high-value crop is being throttled by the ongoing conflict in the Persian Gulf. The Headline Data: The 15% Export Dip For the...
body:
The Indian Kabuli Chana market is facing a structural "Logistics Siege." Despite a healthy harvest, the physical movement of this high-value crop is being throttled by the ongoing conflict in the Persian Gulf. The Headline Data: The 15% Export Dip For the 2025-26 season, India’s Kabuli Chana exports are forecasted to dip by 15%, falling to 0.11 MMT from the previous year's 0.13 MMT. This is not a production failure, but a logistical paralysis. 1. The Production Ledger: Niche but Vital Total Chickpea Output: 11.7 MMT (Rabi 2025-26). Kabuli Chana Share: ~0.5 MMT (only 4.3% of volume, but a major export revenue driver). Hubs: Maharashtra (Latur, Akola) and Madhya Pradesh (Indore, Dewas). The Quality Gap: While the 0.5 MMT volume is intact, "Bold" counts (12mm+) are lower due to February heatwaves, commanding 15-20% size-premium. 2. The Logistics Pincer: A 300% "War Tax" The primary obstacle is the physical blockade of the Strait of Hormuz and Red Sea risks. Freight Explosion: Ocean freight rates to Middle East/Mediterranean hubs have surged by 300%. Voyage Delays: Rerouting via the Cape of Good Hope has added 20–30 days to transit times, triggering massive war-risk insurance premiums. Container Scarcity: A shortage of 20ft boxes at Inland Container Depots (ICDs) like Nagpur and Indore has added a Rs. 2,000/tonne "positioning premium."

3. Market Dynamics & Competition The Middle East Anchor: The UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Iran traditionally absorb 45% of exports. With Ramadan 2026 ending today, an estimated 20,000 tonnes missed the peak demand window and remain "stranded." The Mexican/Russian Squeeze: Mexico is outcompeting Indian 12mm+ in high-end markets, while Russia is dumping smaller calibres at a 10% discount. Currency Neutralization: The 94 INR/USD exchange rate provides an 4-8% buffer, but this has been entirely swallowed by the freight hike. 4. The Mandi Reality: Domestic Price Divergence Logistics issues are creating a "Price Divorce" between origin and destination. Mandi Prices: Trading between Rs. 7,600 and Rs. 8,000 per quintal in Indore and Jalgaon. Exporters' Retreat: Unable to pass on freight costs, exporters have lowered bid prices, forcing farmers to hold back stocks (arrivals are down 25% YoY). Strategic Takeaway India’s 0.5 MMT Kabuli crop is currently a "stranded asset." Without a reopen of shipping lanes, the domestic market faces a potential glut of export-grade bold seeds, which could crash local mandi prices by 12–15% by Q3. The world wants Indian Kabuli, but the map is standing in the way. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_kabulichana-agriexports-globaltrade- activity-7440685204207443968-UW5j?u
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The U.S. Economy: Soft Landing Just Hit a Wall!!
excerpt: For the past year, the global narrative has been obsessed with the "soft landing"—the idea that central banks could tame inflation without breaking the back of the real economy. But as we move through March 2026, the data suggests that the landing is going to...
body:
For the past year, the global narrative has been obsessed with the "soft landing"—the idea that central banks could tame inflation without breaking the back of the real economy. But as we move through March 2026, the data suggests that the landing is going to be far harder than anticipated. We aren't just looking at a "cooling" economy; we are watching the structural pillars of U.S. growth buckle under the weight of geopolitical reality. The signs are no longer subtle. They are flashing red across every major dashboard. 1. The Energy Tax and the Consumer Breaking Point History is a ruthless teacher. Nearly every U.S. recession since 1945 has been preceded by a sharp spike in energy costs. With Brent crude entrenched between $110 and $120 per barrel due to the escalating conflict in the Middle East, the "energy tax" on the American consumer has reached a tipping point. When the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index falls to 55.5—a level historically reserved for deep crises—discretionary spending doesn't just slow down; it evaporates. In an economy where 70% of GDP is driven by consumption, a demoralized consumer is a precursor to a contraction. 2. The Labor Market’s Signal We have transitioned from "labor hoarding" to active job shedding. The loss of 92,000 jobs in February 2026 wasn't just a statistical blip; it was a systemic shock. With unemployment ticking up to 4.4%, we have effectively triggered the "Sahm Rule" logic—where a 0.5% rise in the unemployment rate from its cycle low almost always signals the start of a recession. The cracks are deepest in the service sector, where the PMI has plummeted to 51.7, indicating an engine that is nearly out of fuel.

3. The "De-Inversion" Trap For years, the inverted yield curve was dismissed as a "broken" indicator. However, the most dangerous moment isn't when the curve inverts—it’s when it de-inverts. As the spread between 10-year and 2-year Treasuries turns positive again (now at +0.48%), it signals that the market is finally pricing in a rapid economic deceleration. This "normalization" usually happens in the final weeks or months before the official start of a recession. 4. The Inflation-Growth Paradox The Federal Reserve is currently trapped. Traditionally, a weakening job market would trigger rate cuts. But with PCE inflation projected to rebound to 3.5% due to supply chain disruptions in the Persian Gulf and skyrocketing maritime insurance premiums, the Fed cannot blink. We are entering a period of "stagflationary pressure" where the tools to fix growth only worsen inflation, and vice versa. The Bottom Line for Business Leaders This is not the time for aggressive expansion but to hold cash, the only buffer against rising input costs. The recession isn't "maybe" anymore. It's at the corner, and it’s time to prepare accordingly. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/globaleconomy-recession2026-energycrisis- share-7440780590481698816-zP3C
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Great Lentil Divergence: The Global Glut Won’t Crash Indian Mandis!!
excerpt: The global agricultural stage is currently playing host to a fascinating paradox. On one side, we have a "mountain of masoor (lentils)" building up; on the other, a domestic Indian market that refuses to blink. If you’re looking at headline global prices and...
body:
The global agricultural stage is currently playing host to a fascinating paradox. On one side, we have a "mountain of masoor (lentils)" building up; on the other, a domestic Indian market that refuses to blink. If you’re looking at headline global prices and expecting a dip in Indian mandis, you’re missing the structural "ballast" keeping our local markets steady. The 2025–26 Masoor season is defying the gravity of a global surplus. The Global "Mountain of Masoor" The numbers out of the export hubs are staggering. Canada is sitting on a total production of roughly 3.4 million tons (MMT), with red lentils alone accounting for 1.8 MMT. Combine that with Australia’s robust forecast of 1.9 MMT, and you have a global red lentil pool of nearly 3.7 MMT. In a vacuum, this volume would trigger a price freefall. Indeed, we see this reflected in current global offers, with CFR India values hovering around $590 USD/MT. However, the world of commodities doesn’t exist in a vacuum—it exists in a landscape of logistics and currency. The Currency and Freight "Tax" The $590 global price is effectively a mirage for the Indian importer. With the USD to INR exchange rate sitting at 94, the currency depreciation acts as a massive hidden cost. When you layer on the current 10% import duty the landed cost of imported lentils climbs to approximately ₹61,000 per ton. The "Currency and War Tax" means that even as prices at the origin (Canada and Australia) weaken due to the glut, the cost of bringing that supply to an Indian warehouse remains high enough to provide a protective buffer for our farmers.

The Domestic Anchor: Desi Premium & MSP In India, the narrative is driven by quality and policy. The new domestic crop is hitting the mandis at a time when the government has set a firm floor with a ₹70,000/ton MSP. While raw mandi prices are currently floating between ₹63,000 and ₹65,000 per ton, we must account for the "Desi Premium." Domestic Masoor traditionally commands a 5% premium over imported varieties due to milling quality and consumer preference. The 10% discount on MSP also keeps the government interested in building a buffer. The Outlook: Firm vs. Faded As we look ahead, expect a "dual-speed" market: Domestic Masoor: Likely to remain firm, gravitating toward the ₹6,700–₹6,900 range. The combination of the Desi premium and the psychological safety net of the MSP makes a deep correction unlikely. Imported Masoor: Likely to remain range-bound or weak. The global glut ensures there is no shortage of supply, but the high landed costs (FX/Freight) prevent aggressive merchant buying. Imports will act as a "ceiling," preventing domestic prices from spiking too high, but they won't have the momentum to pull the market down. With expected continuation in government policy: a well-supplied global market struggle against the resilient floor of the Indian mandi. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/the-great-lentil-divergence-the-global-glut- share-7440993548037955584-uEoD
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Tur Scarcity Paradox: Why Domestic Prices Defy the Global Abundance?
excerpt: The Indian Pigeon Pea (Tur) market is currently a textbook study in structural scarcity. While global origins are preparing for a massive surplus, the domestic landscape is defined by a widening gap between demand and a shrinking harvest. The Supply-Demand...
body:
The Indian Pigeon Pea (Tur) market is currently a textbook study in structural scarcity. While global origins are preparing for a massive surplus, the domestic landscape is defined by a widening gap between demand and a shrinking harvest. The Supply-Demand Reality Domestic demand is consistently upward of 4.0 MMT annually, driven by its status as a primary protein staple. However, production has fallen by 0.4 MMT to approximately 2.8 MMT. While the government holds a 0.6 MMT buffer, it serves only as a temporary tool for market intervention and a check on price manipulation. To bridge the remaining gap and maintain a healthy pipeline, India’s import requirement is an absolute 1.0 to 1.2 MMT this year—a figure that confirms a fundamentally bullish long-term outlook. Pricing: The "Desi Premium" vs. MSP Current Mandi prices sit at ₹72,000 - ₹75,000 per ton, trailing the ₹80,000 per ton MSP. However, as availability proves lower than expected, we anticipate domestic Tur will gravitate toward the ₹90,000–₹92,000 range. This "Desi Premium" reflects a supply constraint that harvest arrivals from April to June can only temporarily mask. With African supply not hitting our shores until August, the domestic floor remains structurally firm. The Global Glut & Institutionalized Imports Conversely, origin markets are facing a surplus. Myanmar has an estimated exportable surplus of 0.42 MMT, while the East and Horn of Africa boast a combined carry and anticipated production, based on farmers' intention, of 1.4 MMT. Yet, USD/INR constraints, volatile vessel

availability, high logistics/insurance costs, and lack of capital will be keeping the FOB prices range-bound at the origin. To counter global disruptions, India is institutionalizing its dependency through strategic 5-year MoU extensions beyond 2025–26: Myanmar: Securing 250,000 tons of Urad and 200,000 tons of Tur (including an additional 100,000 tons of dal starting in FY 2026-27). Mozambique & Malawi: Renewing commitments for 200,000 tons and 50,000 tons of Tur respectively. Policy & Currency Dynamics To facilitate this 1-1.2 MMT of committed imports flow, the government is maintaining a Zero- Duty regime to ensure availability. Furthermore, to bypass USD strength and liquidity traps, the INR-Kyat trade mechanism with Myanmar is being strengthened, allowing trade to flow despite currency volatility. The Bottom Line India is building a "Pulse Fortress." While the abundant global availability and dedicated MoUs cap the upside for imports, the domestic scarcity ensures a high-value premium for local produce. Not to forget the Yellow Peas Trump Card. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_agribusiness-turmarket-foodsecurity- activity-7441025711122939904-KGXG
author: Know Your Commodity

title: Yellow Peas Duty: Will the Trade Grapevine be Proven Right or Wrong After March 31st?
excerpt: The March 31st deadline is fast approaching, and in Indian trade circles, the "yellow pea grapevine" is buzzing louder than ever. Since January, speculation has swirled that the current 30% import duty might be scrapped to cool off seasonal price spikes. But...
body:
The March 31st deadline is fast approaching, and in Indian trade circles, the "yellow pea grapevine" is buzzing louder than ever. Since January, speculation has swirled that the current 30% import duty might be scrapped to cool off seasonal price spikes. But as we look at the strategic horizon, is this signal or just noise? When we analyze the ground reality, the case for retaining the duty is far more robust than the rumors of its removal. Over the last two years, the government has done an excellent job of walking the tightrope between consumer inflation and farmer welfare. 1. The Mandi Reality Check Domestic arrivals for Tur and Chana are gaining momentum across Indian mandis. However, a significant concern is that prices for both are trading 5-10% below their Minimum Support Price (MSP). Introducing duty-free yellow peas right now would act as a price depressant, hitting farmer income at the exact moment they bring their hard work to market. 2. Safeguarding the Kharif Sowing Season Our farmers aren't just facing market volatility; they are battling a surge in input costs. Geopolitical tensions in the Middle East have pushed up the prices of fuel and fertilizers. If we allow domestic pulse prices to crash now, we drain the purchasing power of farmers just as they prepare for the Kharif season. This could inadvertently discourage Pigeon Pea sowing—the very opposite of our long-term self-sufficiency goals. 3. The Judicial Watch The judiciary has signaled its intent to protect the producer. Only last week, the Supreme Court

asked the government to reconsider the logic of duty-free imports and suggested stakeholder consultations to safeguard pulses farmers. With the next hearing scheduled for May 2026, a sudden shift in policy before consultations would be a significant departure from general norms. 4. A Different Strategic Playbook The 2026 landscape is fundamentally different from 2023. Back then, duty-free yellow peas were a "relief valve" as Pigeon Peas crossed ₹100/kg. Today, the government is better prepared with a 0.6 million ton buffer stock of Pigeon Peas. This allows for surgical interventions to cool retail prices without the "blunt instrument" of imports that could devastate domestic markets. 5. Weak Price Correlation Data shows that over past few months yellow pea CFR prices in USD terms have only seen a moderate 10% rise primarily due to higher freight and insurance cost, while Tur and Urad have jumped over 25% from respective historic lows. This suggests that yellow peas are no longer the "magic bullet" for controlling wider pulse inflation. The Bottom Line: When we weigh the low mandi prices below MSP, the judicial directive, the rising cost of input for the Kharif season, and the availability of specific buffers, the logic is clear. Despite the loud rumors in trade circles, the signals strongly suggest the duty should be retained. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_yellow-peas-duty-will-the-trade- grapevine-activity-7441058405722644480-kGid
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Silent Heartland Siege: Is 2026 the American Farmer’s Breaking Point?
excerpt: As the 2026 spring planting season commences, the serenity of America’s heartland masks a brutal economic reality. We are no longer discussing a "downturn"—we are witnessing an existential crisis. U.S. Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins recently confirmed a...
body:
As the 2026 spring planting season commences, the serenity of America’s heartland masks a brutal economic reality. We are no longer discussing a "downturn"—we are witnessing an existential crisis. U.S. Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins recently confirmed a chilling statistic: 25% of American farmers—one in every four—have entered the planting window without securing fertilizer. The "Just-in-Time" supply chain has finally snapped. The Hormuz Chokehold The conflict in the Middle East has moved from the headlines to the soil with 20-30% of global nitrogen fertilizer flows blocked. For a nation that imports nearly half of its urea, the math is devastating. Supply Shortfall: The U.S. faces a 25% deficit in nitrogen components. Price Volatility: Fertilizer costs have surged 40% in mere 3 weeks. The Yield Cliff: Without nitrogen, corn and wheat yields could plummet by 40%, not only decimating farmers but also triggering a wave of food inflation. The Financial Bloodletting The "Great Consolidation" is accelerating into an extinction event for the independent operator. The numbers tell a story of systemic collapse: Bankruptcies: Chapter 12 filings jumped 46% in 2025 —the second consecutive year of double- digit increases. Farm Closures: Over 175,000 farms have vanished since 2017, with 15,000 lost in 2025 alone. The

total count has dipped to 1.865 million, an 8% drop in just eight years. The Profit Deficit: Lenders forecast that less than 50% of farmers will be profitable in 2026. The Debt Trap Net farm income is projected at $153.4 billion—a staggering $48 billion collapse (24%) from the 2022 record high. Meanwhile, farm debt has hit an all-time high of $624.7 billion. With interest rates remaining elevated, interest expenses alone will drain $33 billion from rural balance sheets this year. The Distress is Universal From rice farmers facing losses to dairy and poultry sectors bleeding red, the crisis is universal. Many producers are surviving on off-farm jobs, yet this often creates a "Catch-22" that disqualifies them from certain bankruptcy protections, forcing outright land sales and the hollowing out of rural tax bases. The Bottom Line This crisis was ignited by geopolitics, but the fuel was decades of thin margins and record debt. Without bold intervention—domestic fertilizer incentives, trade stabilization, and a fundamental rethink of farm credit—America risks losing the independent backbone of its food system forever. Is the U.S. farm sector entering a "generational downturn" that will permanently shift production to corporate mega-farms, or can policy interventions save the family operator? https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_usagri-farmcrisis2026-fertilizershock- activity-7441116661149196288-6_1n
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The $150 Breaking Point: Is the Oil Engine About to Seize?
excerpt: 14 days back, we analyzed the dip to $90 as a "Geopolitical Mirage"—a sentiment-driven pause in a structural bull market. The "Iron Cap" of $120 we identified is no longer a ceiling; it might become a launchpad if the middle east crisis continue. If the...
body:
14 days back, we analyzed the dip to $90 as a "Geopolitical Mirage"—a sentiment-driven pause in a structural bull market. The "Iron Cap" of $120 we identified is no longer a ceiling; it might become a launchpad if the middle east crisis continue. If the current conflict does not find a off-ramp, the market is staring at a move into the $140–$150 range towards the end of Q2 2026. But make no mistake: $150 oil is an outlier. The world doesn’t "use" $150 oil; it breaks under it. The Timeline of Pain The math of the "supply hole" is turning predatory. With the Strait of Hormuz—the artery for 20% of global seaborne crude—effectively compromised, we are following a non-linear escalation path: 1 Month of Disruption: $105-$110/bbl (Where we are today) 2 Months of Disruption: $120-$125/bbl (Our Q2 trajectory) 3 Months of Disruption: $135-$140/bbl (The pre disruption zone) 4 Months of Disruption: $145-$150/bbl (The total collapse zone) While SPR releases and tactical "de-sanctioning" have acted as band-aids, they cannot plug a physical deficit of this magnitude. As Asia "pre-loads" its reserves, the rest of the world is left bidding for dwindling cargoes and already suffering oil infrastructure in Middle East. The $120–$150 "Dead Zone" Demand destruction is already visible at $110, but the $120–$150 range is where the global economy enters a forced coma.

This isn't just about expensive fuel; it’s a total structural realignment: 1. The Return of Remote Survival: Approximately 80% of global travel and 60% of daily commute is non-essential. At $140, the "convenience" of movement vanishes. Remote work will return not as a health measure, but as a mandatory financial hedge. Mass transports will become attractive even if with inconvenience as affordability clicks in. 2. The "Distance Tax" & Regionalism: Explosive freight and bunker costs will make long-distance imports prohibitive. We will witness the death of hyper-globalization. If a commodity isn't produced regionally, the "distance tax" will render it unmarketable. Trade is shrinking into localized blocs. 3. Industrial Paralysis: High-energy sectors—Steel, Cement, Ceramics, Chemicals—cannot sustain production at these levels. We are anticipating a massive dip in industrial output as the cost of production exceeds the consumer’s ability to pay. 4. The Substitution Trap: Unlike 2008 or 2014, Natural Gas is suffering the same fate as oil. With no "cheap" alternative, the pivot to Ethanol, Solar, and Wind has moved from an ESG initiative to a survival requirement for corporate solvency. The Verdict We may hit $150 by June, but it contains the seeds of its own destruction. A move to $150 triggers a demand collapse so violent that the price is forced back down by the sheer absence of buyers. The $120 breach, if it happens, will be the most significant economic event of 2026. We will be trading the limits of global endurance. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_the-150-breaking-point-is-the-oil-engine- activity-7441372050612600832-NYkz
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The 94 Breach: Navigating the Rupee’s Structural Descent to 98!!
excerpt: On March 4, 2026, we identified the breach of 92 as a warning signal—a "gravity-defying descent with 94 immediate station and 98 year end destination" that many dismissed as temporary volatility. Today, that signal has matured into a structural reality. With...
body:
On March 4, 2026, we identified the breach of 92 as a warning signal—a "gravity-defying descent with 94 immediate station and 98 year end destination" that many dismissed as temporary volatility. Today, that signal has matured into a structural reality. With the pair now trading beyond 94, as forecasted, has reached ahead of schedule. We are no longer discussing a currency fluctuation; we are witnessing a fundamental re-rating of the Indian Rupee. As the global energy complex flirts with a move toward $120 immediately and $150 possibly, the path from 94 to 98 is becoming a mathematical high probability. Here is the economic anatomy of the next phase of this descent. 1. The Dollar Vacuum and the Oil Link The primary driver of the move to 94 is the widening "supply hole" in the energy market. With oil prices pushing toward the $120, India’s trade deficit is under systemic duress. Crude might add approximately $70 billion to India’s Current Account Deficit (CAD) in FY 2026-27. At current levels, Indian refiners are facing a "Dollar Vacuum." The necessity to secure high-cost prompt cargoes in a volatile market creates a relentless demand for USD that far outstrips typical commercial flows. This is the engine driving the pair toward 95 in the immediate term. 2. The Failure of Yield Differentials In our previous analysis, we noted that capital seeks safety, not just yield. Despite a hawkish stance from the RBI, the "spread" between Indian and US yields is narrowing. As the US Dollar Index (DXY) remains the world’s primary "fire shelter," capital is exiting emerging markets at an accelerated pace. The relentless sell-side pressure from foreign portfolio investors is neutralizing

domestic efforts to stabilize the currency. 3. The RBI’s Orderly Retreat The intervention strategy has shifted. While the RBI successfully managed the slide from 90 to 92, defending a specific level against a global geopolitical tide is unsustainable. We are seeing a move from "defending a price" to "managing the velocity of the fall." The central bank is focused on preventing a "panic-driven" spiral, but they are essentially allowing the Rupee to find its new market-clearing level—which currently appears to be well beyond 95. 4. The 98 Destination: A Structural Inevitability? The path to 98 is paved by two emerging economic factors: Imported Inflation: As the Rupee weakens, the cost of fertilizers, food, electronics, and energy spikes. This forces a contraction in industrial output, further weakening the economic base that supports the currency. The Regionalization Pivot: As the "distance tax" on global trade makes long-distance imports prohibitive, India’s export competitiveness is being tested by high logistics costs. If energy prices sustain their rally, the 98 level might not wait till year end. An the inevitable destination for a currency caught in a "permacrisis" of energy and capital flight. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_the-94-breach-navigating-the-rupees- structural-activity-7441388134975578112-JgYN
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Great Brazilian Sugar Washout of 2026 is Happening Right Now!!
excerpt: Forget the early 2025 forecasts of a massive Brazilian sugar surplus. The theoretical food vs. fuel dynamic at the mill gate has exploded into a physical reality. The highly discussed "structural option" to flip cane allocation from sugar exports to domestic...
body:
Forget the early 2025 forecasts of a massive Brazilian sugar surplus. The theoretical food vs. fuel dynamic at the mill gate has exploded into a physical reality. The highly discussed "structural option" to flip cane allocation from sugar exports to domestic ethanol is no longer a risk—it is the active trade flow. The washouts have started, and they are violent. The Perfect Storm: Rock-Bottom Sugar vs. Historic-High Ethanol The economic calculation driving this pivot is simple and brutal. Let's look at the triggers: Sugar’s February Collapse: Up until the end of last month, global sugar prices were at rock bottom. On February 12, 2026, benchmark US Sugar #11 futures plummeted to 13.34 cents per pound—the lowest price recorded since October 2020. Millions of tonnes of Brazilian export contracts were booked at these depressed, highly reduced price levels. Historically High Ethanol Prices: While sugar was crashing, Brazil’s internal ethanol market was exploding. Throughout early 2026, prices have remained at historically high levels, currently up 7.4% year-over-year and rising 5.0% over just the last three months. Severe Ethanol Scarcity: This pricing power is driven by an absolute shortage of fuel supply. As of mid-February, Brazilian hydrous ethanol stocks were 22% lower than the same period last year, while anhydrous stocks were down 24%. The Policy Match in the Powder Keg The Brazilian government just applied the final accelerant. To secure local fuel supplies, the ethanol blend rate in gasoline was recently raised from 27% to 30%. Furthermore, Brasília is reportedly considering a further emergency increase to 35%.

This mandatory demand boom, colliding with historically low stocks and high prices, means mills can make significantly more money producing fuel for local tanks than fulfilling cheap sugar export bags booked last month. The Washout Logic For a mill, the math is inescapable. It is highly profitable to pay the "wash-fee" (contractual penalty) to cancel a sugar export contract booked at 13-14 cents, flip the cane to ethanol, and sell into the short, high-priced domestic fuel market. This dynamic is tightening the effective global physical sugar flow right now, regardless of Conab’s earlier bumper-crop forecasts of 45.9 million tons, a 40-41 million tons looks more practical. If your 2026 procurement strategy relies on assumed "Brazilian abundance," your model is broken. Stop watching the crop forecasts. Watch the ethanol parity and the rising count of washed-out contracts. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_sugartrade-biofuels-agribusiness- activity-7441397248493625344-PuuT
author: Know Your Commodity

title: TRADE ALERT: DON'T SPECULATE ON YELLOW PEAS MISINFORMATION!!
excerpt: The pulses trade is currently being overwhelmed by artificial buzz regarding Yellow Peas. Over the past week, multiple platforms have exploded with chatter ranging from imminent duty removal to dire predictions of inflationary pressures if policies remain...
body:
The pulses trade is currently being overwhelmed by artificial buzz regarding Yellow Peas. Over the past week, multiple platforms have exploded with chatter ranging from imminent duty removal to dire predictions of inflationary pressures if policies remain unchanged, leading to pure panic mongering. Unfortunately, vested interests are once again using Yellow Peas as a vehicle for speculation and market manipulation for a very simple structural reason: much like oil, India imports a massive 80%+ of its Yellow Peas demand. Furthermore, supply is concentrated in limited origins—Canada and Russia. With Russia finding China a more lucrative market, and prohibitively high tariffs facing Canadian peas in China, default dynamics leave India as Canada's largest critical market. The trade must confront historical reality over planted noise. In just the last 8 months, we have broken the myth of imminent policy change news nine distinct times. While we analyzed yesterday why a duty reduction is highly unlikely under current circumstances, today’s alert is about a larger, fundamental point: why is there so much speculative misinformation when the world is going through a broader crisis? Are we behaving as mature traders or scavengers? Analysts are free to put forward their analysis on why governments should or should not change policy and the implications thereof. Journalists can and should share what they are hearing from

their sources. Think tanks, associations, and lobbyists can present their viewpoints to the government and public. However, when misinformation is spread as if parties have an explicit heads-up before others regarding policy changes, traders must become extremely cautious. It is a known fact that leaks in the current Indian government are practically non-existent. Time and again, those claiming to have "seen files" or that a specific notification is "coming on such-and-such day" have been proven wrong. Remember, even a broken clock shows the correct time twice a day; relying on a lucky guess is not a trading strategy. Exercise caution, trade with ethics, and show maturity. Chasing a quick buck through speculation carries far more risk than most can chew. If the last three years in the pulses market have shown anything, it is that speculators have burnt their homes far more often than they have built utopian palaces. Stay smart. Trade with insights, not impulse. Ethics must prevail over greed.
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The $1.2 Billion "Hormuz Grain Tax": Is the Gulf’s Breadbasket Shifting West?
excerpt: The "Price of Bread" in the Gulf has suddenly become a masterclass in maritime disruption and geopolitical risk. As we navigate the fallout of the current regional tensions following the blockade on February 28, the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) is...
body:
The "Price of Bread" in the Gulf has suddenly become a masterclass in maritime disruption and geopolitical risk. As we navigate the fallout of the current regional tensions following the blockade on February 28, the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) is witnessing a stark divergence in food security. While global headlines remain fixated on energy flows, the real "ground-level" crisis is being fought in grain silos and bulk carrier manifests. For the "maritime-locked" quartet —the UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, and Kuwait—the de facto closure of the Strait of Hormuz is a direct hit to the regional breadbasket. These four nations rely on the Strait for nearly 90% of their bulk grain imports, and the current bottleneck is creating a localized delivery crisis of historic proportions. The issue today isn't a global grain shortage—global stocks are actually at a five-year high—it is a logistics and insurance wall. Over 20 grain vessels are currently stationary, landlocked by the total withdrawal of War Risk Insurance for transits through the Strait. In Kuwait and Qatar, the retail price of imported Wheat and Rice has surged by 25% to 35% in just the last three weeks. These economies, built on highly efficient "just-in-time" inventory models, are now tapping into strategic reserves faster than they can be replenished. The Quartet represents a massive, near- total dependency, with an estimated 2026 annual import requirement of approximately 6.2 Million Metric Tons (MMT) of grain. This volume includes nearly 3 MMT of Wheat and 1.8 MMT of Rice that now essentially represents the "Hormuz Risk Volume" which must be rerouted at a staggering premium.

Conversely, Saudi Arabia and Oman are providing a blueprint for regional resilience through geographical "safety valves." Saudi Arabia has successfully diverted the majority of its grain procurement to its Red Sea ports, specifically Jeddah and Yanbu, bypassing the chokepoint entirely. Meanwhile, Oman sits on the Indian Ocean, receiving Indian rice and Australian wheat through Salalah and Duqm without ever entering volatile Gulf waters. While the Quartet scrambles, grain inflation in Saudi and Oman remains contained in the single digits. To bridge the gap, traders are pivoting to an expensive "Land Bridge." Moving a 40ft container from Jeddah or Salalah to Dubai or Kuwait now carries an emergency premium of $3,000 to $5,000. On a per-ton basis, this trucking shift adds $100 to $180 to the cost of grain. When you combine the 30% war risk surcharge with these trucking premiums, the "bypass tax" adds another $30 to $50 per ton. Collectively, the Quartet might end up effectively paying an "Insecurity Premium" of nearly $1.2 Billion just to keep the grains supply flowing. This crisis proves that food security is now a function of Port Redundancy. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_foodsecurity-gcc-supplychain- activity-7442080463390920704-6BVb
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Procurement Pivot: Will the Pulse Basket Hold its Base?
excerpt: The Indian pulses market is navigating a high-stakes tug-of-war between a record-breaking harvest and the government’s procurement machinery. With mandi arrivals hitting peak momentum across the heartland, the "procurement window" has transitioned from a mere...
body:
The Indian pulses market is navigating a high-stakes tug-of-war between a record-breaking harvest and the government’s procurement machinery. With mandi arrivals hitting peak momentum across the heartland, the "procurement window" has transitioned from a mere safety net to the primary architect of the price floor. The market is currently deciphering whether the government's stance will be aggressive enough to mop up the surplus or lukewarm, leaving farmers at the mercy of a supply-heavy open market. Tur (Pigeon Peas): The Resilience of the New Base Tur remains the most resilient player in the pulses basket, anchored by the government's 100% procurement commitment under the Aatmanirbharta Mission. In key hubs like Akola (Maharashtra) and Gulbarga (Karnataka), wholesale prices are currently hovering between ₹7,940 and ₹8,180 per quintal. With the MSP set at ₹8,000, the market is effectively trading at or slightly above par. This suggests that the government’s guarantee and anticipated tur short crop is successfully acting as a psychological floor. However, for this stability to hold throughout the harvest, state agencies must maintain an active buying presence to absorb early high-moisture arrivals that private millers typically discount. Chana (Desi Chickpeas): The High-Volume Battleground Chana is the most vulnerable to volume shocks this season, driven by a massive 13.5 MMT production estimate. In the mandis of Madhya Pradesh—including Indore, Ashta, and Gwalior— wholesale rates are currently averaging between ₹5,250 and ₹5,650 per quintal, marginally below the MSP of ₹5,875. This gap is the critical zone. The government has signaled an aggressive target of 2.3 MT to rebuild depleted national buffers. If NAFED and NCCF accelerate their "vacuum" operation now, prices will likely recover toward the MSP. If procurement remains

lukewarm or bogged down by logistics, the impending April arrival peak could drive prices down toward the ₹5,100 level. Masoor (Lentil): The Red Zone Masoor is currently facing the sharpest downward pressure relative to its support price. In Uttar Pradesh (Lalitpur, Bareilly, Kanpur), whole raw Masoor is trading in the ₹6,100–₹6,450 range, significantly trailing the ₹7,000 MSP. Despite a record production year, the lack of a secondary buyer of scale beyond the government makes this crop highly sensitive. While procurement has begun in pockets, the market in UP is desperate for a massive, aggressive entry by state agencies to prevent a post-harvest price rout. The Bottom Line The pulses market is at a strategic crossroads. An aggressive procurement over the next 60 days will set a higher base for the 2026-27 season and reinforce farmer confidence in the "Aatmanirbharta" goals. Conversely, a lukewarm response will not only put downward pressure on prices in the short term but risk a acreage shift away from pulses in the next planting cycle. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/the-procurement-pivot-will-the-pulse-basket- share-7442198906643464192-Ymud
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Nitrogen Ceiling: Why Corn’s $181.50 Per Ton Jump is a Warning Shot?
excerpt: Monday’s 1.1% jump in corn futures to a two-week high of $4.61 per bushel ($181.50 per metric ton) is a warning shot fired directly from the Strait of Hormuz. For every trader and producer in the grain complex, the mathematics of the 2026 harvest have...
body:
Monday’s 1.1% jump in corn futures to a two-week high of $4.61 per bushel ($181.50 per metric ton) is a warning shot fired directly from the Strait of Hormuz. For every trader and producer in the grain complex, the mathematics of the 2026 harvest have shifted. We are no longer just trading on weather; we are trading on the availability of the nitrogen molecules required to grow a crop. To understand the magnitude, look at the trendlines. At $181.50 per ton, corn is significantly more expensive than just last month, when prices averaged $4.40 per bushel ($173.22 per ton) in late February—a 4.8% surge. We are also trending above the $4.55 per bushel ($179.12 per ton) mark seen this time last year. Looking at the 52-week volatility, we have swung from a low of $3.91 per bushel ($153.93 per ton) to a high of $4.95 per bushel ($194.87 per ton). This rally is aggressively testing those upper resistance levels as the "Middle East Premium" gets baked into the December new-crop contracts. The $660 Urea Wall & The USDA Acreage Swap The driver is a de facto blockade land-locking 20% of global urea and ammonia capacity. Urea prices have now hit $660 per metric ton, a sharp surge that is fundamentally altering planting decisions. According to the latest USDA Agricultural Outlook, American farmers are already orchestrating a massive shift. The USDA projects corn plantings to drop by 4.8 million acres to 94 million acres in 2026, while soybean acreage is forecast to climb by nearly 4 million acres to 85 million acres.

This is a direct response to the "Fertilizer Chokehold." With urea at $660 per ton, corn—the most nitrogen-intensive major crop—is losing its competitive edge. In fact, one ton of urea now costs US farmers the equivalent of 143 bushels of corn, a staggering increase from the 75-bushel equivalent seen back in December 2025. The Yield Risk Traders are buying up December new-crop corn in anticipation of lower yields. The logic is simple: if farmers cannot secure or afford the nitrogen needed for peak output, the overall US crop will shrink. While the Southern Hemisphere’s summer crops in Brazil and Argentina remain insulated for now (as nutrient application was completed before the escalation), the risk for the Northern Hemisphere's April–May planting window is acute. In today’s market, the $660 per ton urea price and the USDA-projected shift to soybeans are more influential metrics than the rain. It is important to redraw 2026 strategy prepared for a "Nitrogen-Deficient" yield and a record soybean surge, as one can't be trading on 2025 assumptions. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_cornfutures-fertilizercrisis-supplychain- activity-7442218659110035456-cWK6
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Shipping Surcharge Scapegoat: Market Physics Trump Political Optics!!
excerpt: Popular sentiment often seeks a villain in times of crisis, and right now, global shipping lines are the primary target. Following the maritime upheaval of February 28, 2026, and the subsequent de facto blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, regulators in India...
body:
Popular sentiment often seeks a villain in times of crisis, and right now, global shipping lines are the primary target. Following the maritime upheaval of February 28, 2026, and the subsequent de facto blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, regulators in India and Pakistan have labeled retrospective "War Risk" and "Emergency Conflict Surcharges" (ECS) as opportunistic. But as an economist looking at the "market physics" of global trade, we must ask: Is blaming the carriers actually fair, or is it a dangerous distraction from the reality of supply chain emergency? The argument that surcharges shouldn't apply to cargo already in transit is emotionally resonant but economically flawed. Risk is not a static photo taken at the moment of departure; it is a live, evolving variable. A vessel that set sail on February 20 is just as exposed to the March 2026 insurance hike as one leaving today. When P&I Clubs and hull insurers spike premiums by 400% overnight due to active hostilities, that cost hits the carrier’s P&L instantly, regardless of when the "dotted line" on the bill of lading was signed. Furthermore, the decision to reroute around the Cape of Good Hope adds 14 days and nearly $1.5 million in unplanned fuel costs per voyage. These are not "future" problems—they are immediate, cash-flow-heavy operational realities. We must also address the "Profitability Paradox." Critics often point to the record earnings of shipping lines during the 2024-25 cycle, suggesting they should "absorb" current losses. This is a logical fallacy we would never apply to other sectors. We don't ask a grain trader to sell wheat at a loss in 2026 simply because they had a bumper year in 2024. In a free market, capital-intensive industries must remain solvent through the volatility they navigate. Expecting private maritime

entities to act as a non-profit "shock absorber" for global geopolitics is a fundamental misunderstanding of corporate fiduciary duty. If you signed the contract, the clauses for "Liberty" and "Force Majeure" are there for exactly this reason: to ensure the vessel survives the crisis to sail another day. Perhaps the greatest danger, however, lies in heavy-handed government intervention. Global shipping is the ultimate mobile asset class. If regulators in specific regions mandate price caps or "cease and desist" orders on surcharges, they risk a "Market Flight." Carriers have a duty to move their vessels to routes—like the Trans-Pacific or Asia-Europe—where they can recover their full operational costs. If a route becomes an artificial loss-maker due to local price controls, capacity will simply evaporate. Let's stop pretending that shipping lines can—or should—subsidize the cost of global conflict. In the current maritime geography, the "Cape Tax" is real, and someone has to pay it. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/the-shipping-surcharge-scapegoat-market- share-7442231854348533760-s3l3
author: Know Your Commodity

title: EU-Australia Deal: Strategic Milestone Ending The Eight-Year Trade Wait!!
excerpt: On March 24, 2026, the long-awaited conclusion of the EU-Australia Free Trade Agreement (FTA) marked a historic pivot in global trade geography. After eight years of grueling negotiations and several high-profile "walk-away" moments, Prime Minister Anthony...
body:
On March 24, 2026, the long-awaited conclusion of the EU-Australia Free Trade Agreement (FTA) marked a historic pivot in global trade geography. After eight years of grueling negotiations and several high-profile "walk-away" moments, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese and President Ursula von der Leyen have finally signed off on a deal that promises to reshape the economic corridor between the Indo-Pacific and the European continent. While the political rhetoric celebrates a win for "rules-based trade" and "strategic autonomy," a deeper dive into the numbers reveals a complex tapestry of massive wins for high-tech sectors, critical minerals, and agricultural. The deal is undeniably ambitious. It aims to remove over 99% of tariffs on EU exports to Australia and allow around 98% of Australian exports by current value to enter the EU duty-free once fully implemented. The EU has used the FTA to lock in improved, long-term access to Australian lithium, manganese, cobalt and other CRMs, underpinning its green transition and reducing single-supplier dependence. The economic impact is tangible, with an expected multi- billion euro increase in EU GDP by 2030 and a projected 33% surge in annual exports over the next decade. In the agriculture sector for Australian grains, horticulture, and wine, the agreement is a breakthrough. Tariffs on key exports such as wheat, barley and tree nuts, including macadamias, are to be eliminated or significantly reduced over the implementation period opening a high- value door that has been effectively bolted shut for decades. The Geographical Indications (GI) consensus ensures that the EU secured protection for hundreds of food names and spirit GIs,

meaning terms like Roquefort and Champagne are now strictly European intellectual property in the Australian market. In exchange, Australian producers secured "grandfathering" rights for existing users of certain terms like Feta. The real friction, however, lies in the Red Meat sector. A beef quota of only 30,600 tonnes and sheep meat capped at 25,000 tonnes, the Australian red meat sector sees it as a trickle than a flood. When you consider that 45% of this beef volume will still attract a 7.5% tariff, it’s clear that the EU's "sensitive sector" safeguards remained incredibly rigid. Ultimately, this FTA is a textbook example of modern trade-offs. It prioritizes digital trade, innovation mobility, and energy security over traditional bulk-commodity parity. While the agricultural "sensitive" products are shielded by carefully calibrated quotas, the broader economy gains. We are moving toward a future where "trade" is as much about the movement of lithium and data as it is about beef and wheat. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_euaustraliafta-globaltrade-agribusiness- activity-7442308164659814400-KfJd
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Rice Catastrophe: A Bumper Harvest with Nowhere to Go!!
excerpt: The global rice market is currently witnessing a "perfect storm" that threatens to impact India’s dominance as the world’s top exporter. While 2026 was heralded as the year of a great recovery following the lifting of export curbs, it has instead transformed...
body:
The global rice market is currently witnessing a "perfect storm" that threatens to impact India’s dominance as the world’s top exporter. While 2026 was heralded as the year of a great recovery following the lifting of export curbs, it has instead transformed into a logistical and geopolitical nightmare. We are staring at a paradoxical disaster: record-breaking production colliding with a paralyzed delivery mechanism abroad. The numbers are alarming. Approximately 400,000 tonnes of Indian rice are currently stranded at ports or idling at sea, unable to reach key Middle Eastern markets due to the escalating conflict in the Gulf. For a nation that accounts for 40% of global rice trade, this is a systemic strangulation. The "Ramadan Surge" that exporters bank on every year simply vanished. Instead, we are seeing domestic Basmati prices saw a dip of 10% per ton already as panic sets in. The domestic prices are falling at a far elevated pace. The math is simple and terrifying: if these stranded consignments are diverted back into the domestic market, we will trigger a supply glut that will crash prices, wiping out the margins for 7,000+ exporters and millions of farmers who invested heavily in this "bumper" crop. While global FOB prices for non-basmati varieties have hit historic, decadal lows—often seen in the $330–$350 per ton range—the "Freight Tax" is killing the trade. Sea freight costs have surged by an additional $400 per ton. This isn't just an incremental increase; it is a de facto tariff that, in some cases, represents nearly 100% of the grain’s value. We are effectively paying an "Insecurity Premium" that makes Indian rice uncompetitive, even as regional rivals like Pakistan see their volumes plummet by over 35% and Thailand’s exports to West Asia come to a grinding halt.

Adding to this volatility is the "Red Tape War." China’s recent rejection of Indian shipments over alleged "GMO presence"—despite India’s absolute ban on commercial GM food crops—is a calculated move of strategic leverage. While competitors like Vietnam and Thailand face no such selective scrutiny, India is being targeted just as China’s import needs are projected to rise to 3.1 million tonnes. It is geopolitical maneuvering disguised as food safety, and it is costing us millions in lost revenue. The crisis extends beyond the silo to the soil. Our next sowing season is under threat as the conflict chokes off Urea and LNG imports from the Gulf. A prolonged blockade of the Strait of Hormuz will hit the very inputs required to maintain our "Bumper" status. 2026 is the year India might miss its export targets by a massive margin in value and to a certain extent in volume. Exporters are facing zero profits, traders are sitting on stagnant stock, and the government is caught between managing domestic inflation and a collapsing export sector. The "Rice King" is being cornered by forces far beyond the farm gate. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_the-rice-catastrophe-a-bumper-harvest- with-activity-7442479389780647936-Uthw
author: Know Your Commodity

title: No Gas, No Oil, No Fertilizer: Hormuz Crisis!!
excerpt: The WTO/AXSMarine Strait of Hormuz Trade Tracker confirms a systemic collapse in maritime traffic following the February 28, 2026, closure. The data reflects a transition from geopolitical tension to a total physical blockade of one of the world’s most vital...
body:
The WTO/AXSMarine Strait of Hormuz Trade Tracker confirms a systemic collapse in maritime traffic following the February 28, 2026, closure. The data reflects a transition from geopolitical tension to a total physical blockade of one of the world’s most vital trade arteries. Key Data Insights: The Energy & Fertilizer "Break": Outbound shipments of Crude Oil, LNG, and fertilizer precursors (ammonia and sulphur) plummeted to near zero by March 2. This immediate halt has removed significant daily volumes from the global market, directly fueling the 30%+ surge in urea prices and the sharp rise in energy futures. The cessation of fertilizer inputs is particularly critical for Northern Hemisphere planting windows. The Inbound Agricultural Lag: While outbound traffic stopped instantly, the Inbound Agricultural Index (measured by discharge date) showed a temporary lag. This reflects the unloading of vessels that entered the Strait before the February 28 deadline. As this "pre-arrival" inventory finishes discharging, the region faces a severe supply-side cliff for food security starting in April. AIS Integrity: While the tracker monitors AIS-traceable vessels, the scale of the drop suggests that commercial shipping at scale has effectively abandoned the route. Any "dark" transits represent a negligible fraction of the former volume. Conclusion: The Strait has ceased to function as a global trade corridor. The total halt in fertilizer precursors and hydrocarbons creates a global multiplier effect, moving the crisis from a logistics delay to a fundamental production and inflation emergency.

Trade Momentum Index (March 24, 2026): Outbound Crude/LNG: Near Zero (Total Halt) Fertilizer Precursors: Near Zero (Supply Chain Break) Inbound Agriculture: Declining (Lagged Disruption)
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The Domino: Russia's Ammonium Nitrate Stop, a Global Alarm!!
excerpt: Russia’s decision to halt all ammonium nitrate (AN) exports till April 21, 2026, is not a temporary logistical tweak; it is the final piece of the "perfect storm" that has officially shattered the global fertilizer market. We must move beyond analyzing this...
body:
Russia’s decision to halt all ammonium nitrate (AN) exports till April 21, 2026, is not a temporary logistical tweak; it is the final piece of the "perfect storm" that has officially shattered the global fertilizer market. We must move beyond analyzing this as a one-month pause and start treating it as a strategic, tier-1 resource mobilization event with profound geopolitical consequences. The Final Chokepoint: Overlapping Crises The significance of this ban is magnified by the timing and the context. Global nitrogen supplies were already in a state of advanced failure. Roughly 24% of global ammonia trade (the key AN precursor) and 43% of urea are already trapped behind the Strait of Hormuz closure. By removing Russia’s dominant 40% trade share of global AN, the global market has effectively lost its single biggest cushion precisely as the Northern Hemisphere enters its critical April spring planting window. Furthermore, Russia’s internal capacity was already under duress. Recent drone strikes on Russian facilities like KuybyshevAzot had already damaged output, with recovery not expected until May. The export ban is less about the duration and more about the timing—a move of absolute agri- nationalism that prioritizes domestic planting security over global contracts. Brazil and India: The Critical Nodes The dominoes are already falling across major global agricultural regions. Brazil is in a critical state. With >90% dependence on Russia for its AN imports, the suspension effectively stalls the supply chain for a nation that acts as a vital global protein and calorie supplier. India faces a complex challenge. Russia provides approximately 33% of India's fertilizer imports.

While India has a 36.5% YoY stock cushion, domestic production is already declining as internal gas supplies are capped at 75%. The "Just-in-Case" strategy is rapidly deflating. The Inflationary Ripple and the Great Acreage Swap For US and EU farmers, the "nitrogen math" is forcing a radical, immediate pivot. Global urea prices have exploded, jumping 77% since mid-December 2025. This might trigger the "Great Acreage Swap." Farmers might mass-pivot from fertilizer-intensive crops like corn to lower-nutrient options like soybeans or pulses to preserve margins. This shift will compress global yields. Every 1% increase in fertilizer prices correlates with a 0.45% rise in global food prices, and we are now looking at a potential 10–15% food price jump by mid-2026. The era of "Just-in-Time" supply chains for global agriculture has officially ended. Is your 2026 strategy optimized for a world of "Nitrogen-Deficient" yields? https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_fertilizercrisis-agrieconomy- globalfoodsecurity-activity-7442537818779725824-AzT5
author: Know Your Commodity

title: Brazil’s Thin Soybean Margins: Production Boom Meets Financial Bust!!
excerpt: Brazil is on the verge of a historic agricultural milestone. Projections for the 2025-26 season indicate a staggering 6.5 billion bushels of soybeans—a record-breaking volume that cements the nation’s status as the undisputed heavyweight of global oilseed...
body:
Brazil is on the verge of a historic agricultural milestone. Projections for the 2025-26 season indicate a staggering 6.5 billion bushels of soybeans—a record-breaking volume that cements the nation’s status as the undisputed heavyweight of global oilseed production. But behind this wall of grain lies a sobering economic reality: Brazilian farmers are staring down their thinnest margins in nearly two decades. This is the "Success Paradox." While production technology and acreage expansion have hit an all-time high, the financial "breakeven" point has moved dangerously close to the surface. Data from the Purdue Center for Commercial Agriculture and recent farmdoc analysis reveal a systemic squeeze that should have every global commodity trader on high alert. The Data Behind the Squeeze In Mato Grosso—the engine room of Brazilian soy—the trend lines are crossing in the wrong direction. According to cost-revenue modeling: Gross Revenue Collapse: From a peak of nearly $800 per acre during the 2021/22 boom, gross revenue has plummeted to a projected $550 per acre for the 2025/26 cycle. Sticky Production Costs: Despite the revenue drop, production costs have remained stubbornly high, plateauing at roughly $540–$550 per acre. The Result: Net margins have effectively evaporated. We are witnessing the lowest profitability levels since the 2006/07 season. Brazilian farmers are essentially "trading dollars," producing record volume just to service existing debt.

Why Is the Giant Stalling? Brazil’s competitiveness is no longer just about yield; it’s about the "Logistics and Basis Trap." The Basis Erosion: Record domestic supply is "heavy-loading" the local infrastructure. With storage at capacity, the local basis has weakened significantly, meaning the price the farmer actually receives is at a massive discount to Chicago futures. The Infrastructure Tax: While U.S. farmers benefit from a mature multi-modal transport system, Brazil’s interior expansion relies on long-haul trucking. High energy costs and the "Freight Tax" from global maritime disruptions are eating up to 30% of the farm-gate value. The Credit Crunch: Higher interest rates in Brazil compared to the U.S. mean that financing this 6.5B bushels crop is significantly more expensive, further hollowing out the net margin. The Global Ripple Effect When the world’s largest exporter hits a profitability wall, the shockwaves are global: The U.S. Pivot: U.S. soybean competitiveness is currently buoyed by superior logistics and domestic crush demand. As Brazil nears breakeven, any further price drop could lead to a sudden contraction in Brazilian acreage for 2027, handing a strategic advantage back to the American Midwest. The Bottom Line: Production is a vanity metric; profitability is the only sanity metric. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_brazils-thin-soybean-margins- production-activity-7442577549773205504-ZYp8
author: Know Your Commodity

title: The 16.5 Cent Ceiling: Has Sugar Run Out of Gas?
excerpt: The global sugar market is trapped in a fascinating paradox. Recently sugar futures surged toward 15.8 cents—the highest level since October 2025. On the surface, the bullish narrative seems unbeatable. But a deeper dive into the numbers, climate physics, and...
body:
The global sugar market is trapped in a fascinating paradox. Recently sugar futures surged toward 15.8 cents—the highest level since October 2025. On the surface, the bullish narrative seems unbeatable. But a deeper dive into the numbers, climate physics, and logistical constraints reveals that this rally is not just extended; it’s likely maxed out. The sugar market has discounted all available good news and is now operating under a hard price ceiling. The False Positive of $150 Oil The entire March rally was fueled not by a sugar deficit, but by the March 4 closure of the Strait of Hormuz and destruction of energy production infrastructure, which stranded 20% of global transit and sent Brent Crude surging past $119 per barrel. In the standard "Ethanol Parity" model, this is pure rocket fuel. High oil makes hydrous ethanol immensely profitable for Brazilian mills, incentivizing them to maximize biofuel output and "squeeze" the global sugar supply. India and Brazil blending is maxed.
